questions.q: Which of the following best describes the primary function of the nuclear pore complex (NPC) in the context of nuclear transport?
options: It generates the Ran-GTP gradient that drives directional transport
It acts as the selective gateway through which all nuclear–cytoplasmic traffic passes
It synthesises importins and exportins for use in nuclear transport
It anchors the nuclear envelope to the endoplasmic reticulum membrane
It degrades misfolded proteins before they enter the nucleus
questions.explain: The NPC is the sole channel through which molecules cross the nuclear envelope, providing both size selectivity and regulated passage. It does not generate the Ran-GTP gradient (that role belongs to Ran-GEF and Ran-GAP) nor does it synthesise transport receptors.

questions.q: Which two classes of macromolecules must cross the nuclear envelope in opposite directions to maintain normal cell function?
options: Lipids and carbohydrates
Histones and cholesterol
RNA and proteins
DNA and ribosomes
Transcription factors and lipid bilayers
questions.explain: RNA is synthesised in the nucleus and exported to the cytoplasm for translation, while proteins (e.g. histones, polymerases, transcription factors) are synthesised in the cytoplasm and imported into the nucleus. DNA does not routinely cross the nuclear envelope, and lipids/carbohydrates are not transported via the NPC in this manner.

questions.q: In the gold-particle electron microscopy experiment, what was the maximum diameter of uncoated gold particles that could enter the nucleus by passive diffusion?
options: 4–5 nm
15 nm
20 nm
50 nm
questions.explain: Uncoated gold particles must be smaller than 8–10 nm to enter the nucleus passively. Particles coated with a nuclear protein could enter up to 20 nm in diameter, demonstrating that the protein coat (NLS-containing) enables active transport beyond the passive diffusion limit.

questions.q: What was the critical control in the gold-particle EM experiment that proved a specific protein motif — not just any coating — was required for nuclear entry of large particles?
options: Adding excess importin to the cytoplasm
Replacing the nuclear protein coat with a cytoplasmic protein coat
Treating coated particles with trypsin before injection
Injecting particles directly into the nucleus
Using particles larger than 50 nm to saturate the NPC
questions.explain: Trypsin digestion of the nuclear protein coat on 20 nm particles prevented nuclear entry, proving that a specific protein sequence (the NLS-containing motif) is responsible, not a non-specific size or coating effect. Without trypsin digestion, one could argue that any protein coating would allow entry.

questions.q: In the in vitro reconstitution experiment using digitonin-permeabilized cells, which result was observed when fluorescently labelled nuclear proteins were added with buffer and purified NLS receptor alone (no cytoplasmic lysate)?
options: Nuclear import occurred at the same rate as with full lysate
Nuclear import occurred at half the rate observed with lysate
Nuclear import was completely abolished (0× relative import)
The nuclear envelope became permeable and import was unregulated
Import occurred but cargo was not released inside the nucleus
questions.explain: Buffer plus purified NLS receptor alone gave 0× import, demonstrating that the receptor alone is insufficient. Additional cytosolic factors from the lysate are required. Cytosol alone gave 9× import, and cytosol plus receptor gave 23× import, showing receptor enhances but cannot replace cytosolic factors.

questions.q: What was the purpose of using digitonin in the in vitro nuclear transport reconstitution experiment?
options: To degrade the nuclear envelope so cargo could diffuse freely
To permeabilize only the plasma membrane, creating semi-intact cells with an intact nuclear envelope
To inhibit Ran-GAP activity and accumulate Ran-GTP in the cytoplasm
To fluorescently label the nuclear proteins before injection
To block FG-repeat interactions in the NPC channel
questions.explain: Digitonin selectively permeabilizes the plasma membrane while leaving the nuclear envelope intact, creating semi-intact cells. This allows researchers to add or remove cytoplasmic components and reconstitute nuclear transport in a controlled manner. It does not degrade the nuclear envelope or affect Ran-GAP.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about GFP-based live-cell imaging of nuclear transport is correct?
options: GFP fusions can only track nuclear import, not export
Roger Tsien used GFP fusions to show nuclear transport is often a regulated and dynamic process
GFP fusions to nuclear proteins always remain permanently in the nucleus after import
GFP fusions proved that NLS sequences are cleaved after nuclear entry
GFP can only be used in fixed cells and cannot track live nuclear transport
questions.explain: Live imaging of GFP-tagged nuclear proteins revealed that transport is regulated and dynamic — proteins can shuttle rapidly between nucleus and cytoplasm in response to signals. GFP does not prove NLS cleavage (NLS is retained), and GFP fusions can track both import and export in live cells.

questions.q: What is a nuclear localization signal (NLS)?
options: A hydrophobic sequence that anchors proteins to the nuclear envelope membrane
A long stretch of negatively charged amino acids that recruits ribosomes
A short stretch of positively charged amino acids that directs proteins to the nucleus
A signal peptide that is cleaved after the protein enters the nucleus
A glycosylation site that marks proteins for nuclear pore insertion
questions.explain: NLS sequences are short stretches of positively charged amino acids — primarily lysine (Lys) and arginine (Arg) — that are recognized by importins to direct proteins to the nucleus. Unlike ER signal peptides, NLS sequences are not cleaved after import; they remain permanently on the protein.

questions.q: Which experiment demonstrated that the NLS is sufficient (not just necessary) for nuclear targeting?
options: Injecting gold particles coated with a nuclear protein into oocytes
Mutating a single Lys residue in the SV40 T-antigen NLS
Fusing the SV40 T-antigen NLS to pyruvate kinase, which then accumulated in the nucleus
Adding trypsin to digest the NLS and observing loss of nuclear entry
Expressing importin in cells lacking endogenous NLS-bearing proteins
questions.explain: Fusing the SV40 T-antigen NLS to pyruvate kinase — a normally cytoplasmic enzyme — was sufficient to redirect it into the nucleus. This demonstrates sufficiency. Necessity was shown by mutating the NLS and observing that the protein remained cytoplasmic.

questions.q: How do importins interact with the nuclear pore complex to translocate cargo?
options: Importins hydrolyse GTP to push through the NPC channel by force
Importins bind and unbind sequentially to FG-repeat regions of channel nucleoporins
Importins open a calcium channel in the nuclear membrane to allow passive entry
Importins covalently attach to scaffold nucleoporins and are pulled through
Importins recruit motor proteins such as kinesin to walk along nuclear pore filaments
questions.explain: Importin–cargo complexes translocate through the NPC by repeatedly binding, dissociating, and rebinding to adjacent FG-repeat regions (disordered sequences) of channel nucleoporins. This ratchet-like mechanism does not require GTP hydrolysis during translocation itself; energy is consumed at the Ran-GTPase cycle level.

questions.q: What happens to the NLS sequence after a nuclear protein is successfully imported into the nucleus?
options: It is cleaved by a nuclear protease immediately after entry
It is phosphorylated to mask it and prevent re-export
It remains permanently on the protein unchanged
It is ubiquitinated and degraded in the nucleus
It is transferred to the importin to guide it back to the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Unlike ER signal peptides, the NLS is NOT cleaved after import — it remains permanently on the protein. This is a common exam trap. ER signal sequences are cotranslationally cleaved, but nuclear targeting signals are retained.

questions.q: The SV40 T-antigen NLS has the sequence Pro–Pro–Lys–Lys–Lys–Arg–Lys–Val. What happens when a single lysine in this motif is mutated to alanine?
options: Nuclear import is enhanced because steric hindrance is reduced
Nuclear import is unaffected because the remaining lysines are sufficient
Nuclear localization is abolished
The protein is redirected to the ER instead of the nucleus
Importin binds more tightly due to reduced electrostatic repulsion
questions.explain: A single Lys mutation in the SV40 T-antigen NLS abolishes nuclear localization, demonstrating the critical requirement for positively charged residues. This highlights that the NLS is a precise signal, not a redundant sequence — loss of even one key charged residue disrupts importin binding.

questions.q: Where are Ran-GEF and Ran-GAP located, and what is the functional consequence of this distribution?
options: Ran-GEF in cytoplasm, Ran-GAP in nucleus — Ran-GDP accumulates in nucleus
Ran-GEF in nucleus, Ran-GAP in cytoplasm — Ran-GTP accumulates in nucleus and Ran-GDP accumulates in cytoplasm
Both Ran-GEF and Ran-GAP are in the nucleus — Ran cycles within the nucleus only
Ran-GEF in nucleus, Ran-GAP in nucleus — Ran-GTP is equal in both compartments
Ran-GEF in cytoplasm, Ran-GAP in cytoplasm — Ran-GDP accumulates in cytoplasm
questions.explain: Ran-GEF (guanine nucleotide exchange factor) is located in the nucleus and converts Ran-GDP to Ran-GTP, so Ran-GTP is high in the nucleus. Ran-GAP (GTPase-activating protein) is in the cytoplasm and converts Ran-GTP to Ran-GDP, so Ran-GDP is high in the cytoplasm. This gradient is the engine of directional transport.

questions.q: During nuclear IMPORT, what is the role of Ran-GTP inside the nucleus?
options: Ran-GTP in the nucleus stabilizes the importin–cargo complex to prevent premature dissociation
Ran-GTP in the nucleus binds importin and causes rapid dissociation of cargo from importin
Ran-GTP in the nucleus phosphorylates the NLS to signal that import is complete
Ran-GTP in the nucleus recruits exportins to remove empty importins from the nucleus
Ran-GTP in the nucleus hydrolyses the NLS to prevent re-import of the same cargo
questions.explain: Inside the nucleus, abundant Ran-GTP binds to importin, causing the importin–cargo complex to rapidly dissociate and release the cargo. This is the mechanism by which cargo is unloaded in the nucleus. Ran-GTP stabilizes the exportin–cargo complex (for export), but has the opposite effect on importins.

questions.q: During nuclear EXPORT, what triggers the dissociation of the exportin–cargo–Ran-GTP ternary complex?
options: Ran-GEF in the nucleus converts Ran-GTP to Ran-GDP, destabilizing the complex
Ran-GAP in the cytoplasm promotes GTP hydrolysis by Ran, converting it to Ran-GDP
Importins compete with exportins for cargo binding in the cytoplasm
Phosphorylation of the NES by cytoplasmic kinases prevents rebinding
The complex spontaneously disassembles when the NES is exposed to cytoplasmic pH
questions.explain: When the exportin–cargo–Ran-GTP complex reaches the cytoplasm, Ran-GAP (located in the cytoplasm) stimulates GTP hydrolysis by Ran, converting Ran-GTP to Ran-GDP. This hydrolysis causes the ternary complex to dissociate, releasing cargo into the cytoplasm. Ran-GEF is in the nucleus and has the opposite function.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the stable ternary complex required for nuclear export?
options: Importin + NLS cargo + Ran-GTP, formed in the cytoplasm
Exportin + NES cargo + Ran-GDP, formed in the nucleus
Exportin + NES cargo + Ran-GTP, formed in the nucleus
Exportin + NLS cargo + Ran-GAP, formed at the NPC
Importin + NES cargo + Ran-GDP, formed in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Inside the nucleus where Ran-GTP is abundant, exportin binds its cargo (which carries a nuclear export signal, NES) and Ran-GTP to form a stable ternary complex. This complex is then competent to traverse the NPC. In the cytoplasm, Ran-GAP hydrolyses GTP and the complex falls apart.

questions.q: Which of the following GTPases is correctly matched with its primary cellular function?
options: Ran — regulates vesicle trafficking between ER and Golgi
Rab — facilitates nuclear import and export of proteins
Ras — regulates vesicle trafficking
Ran — facilitates nuclear transport; Rab — vesicle trafficking; Ras — cell differentiation and growth signalling
Ras — nuclear transport; Ran — cell differentiation; Rab — nuclear export
questions.explain: Ran GTPases facilitate transport to and from the nucleus; Rab GTPases regulate vesicle trafficking; and Ras GTPases are involved in cell differentiation and growth signalling. Each GTPase family has a distinct intracellular role despite sharing the GTPase mechanism.

questions.q: A drug inhibits Ran-GEF activity, completely blocking GDP-to-GTP exchange on Ran. What is the most likely consequence for nuclear import?
options: Import is enhanced because importins can cycle faster without Ran-GTP
Importins enter the nucleus but cannot release their cargo because Ran-GTP is absent
Importins cannot bind their cargo in the cytoplasm because NLS recognition requires Ran-GTP
Export is specifically abolished but import is unaffected
The NPC becomes clogged with cargo–importin complexes in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Ran-GTP in the nucleus is required to trigger dissociation of cargo from importin. If Ran-GEF is inhibited, Ran-GTP cannot be generated in the nucleus; importins enter the nucleus but cannot release their cargo. This traps the importin–cargo complex inside the nucleus and halts further import cycles.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three general mechanisms by which nuclear transport can be regulated?
options: Phosphorylation of amino acids near the NLS or NES
Inhibitory proteins that mask the NLS or NES
Anchoring proteins that retain cargo in the cytosol or another compartment
Proteolytic cleavage of importin inside the nucleus
All three options above (phosphorylation, inhibitory proteins, anchoring) are valid mechanisms
questions.explain: The three mechanisms are: (1) phosphorylation near the NLS/NES to mask or expose it; (2) inhibitory proteins that cover the signal; and (3) anchoring proteins that retain cargo away from the NPC. Proteolytic cleavage of importin is not a recognized regulatory mechanism — cleavage is used for cargo release (e.g. SREBP at the Golgi), not for importin regulation.

questions.q: In the Drosophila embryo, the transcription factor Dorsal is excluded from nuclei on the dorsal side but concentrated in nuclei on the ventral side. This is an example of which regulatory mechanism?
options: Proteolytic cleavage of Dorsal at the Golgi releasing its nuclear domain
Phosphorylation of NLS residues blocking importin binding on the ventral side
Spatial regulation of nuclear import creating a transcription factor gradient along the dorsal-ventral axis
Ran-GEF expression confined to ventral cells generating a local Ran-GTP gradient
Differential expression of exportins on the dorsal side actively removing Dorsal
questions.explain: The Dorsal protein example illustrates how spatially regulated nuclear import can create a morphogen-like transcription factor gradient that patterns the embryo along its dorsal-ventral axis. The mechanism involves inhibitory proteins (e.g. Cactus) retaining Dorsal in the cytoplasm on the dorsal side, not Golgi cleavage or local Ran-GTP differences.

questions.q: Under HIGH cholesterol conditions, how is SREBP kept from entering the nucleus?
options: SREBP is phosphorylated on its NLS by cholesterol-activated kinases
SCAP binds cholesterol and retains SREBP anchored in the ER membrane
Exportins actively export SREBP from the nucleus in response to high cholesterol
Ran-GAP is upregulated by cholesterol, reducing nuclear Ran-GTP
SREBP is ubiquitinated and degraded by proteasomes in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Under high cholesterol, SCAP (an ER membrane protein) binds cholesterol, adopts a conformation that retains the SCAP–SREBP complex in the ER membrane, preventing packaging into transport vesicles. This is an example of anchoring regulation. SREBP nuclear import is not regulated by direct NLS phosphorylation.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of events that allows SREBP to enter the nucleus when cholesterol is LOW?
options: Cholesterol depletion → SCAP phosphorylates SREBP NLS → importin binds → nuclear entry
Cholesterol depletion → SCAP conformation changes → SCAP+SREBP packaged into vesicles → delivered to Golgi → proteolytic cleavage → SREBP domain released → nuclear entry
Cholesterol depletion → SREBP is dephosphorylated → NLS exposed → importin binds → nuclear entry
Cholesterol depletion → SCAP degrades → SREBP released from ER → diffuses to nucleus
Cholesterol depletion → Ran-GEF activity increases → more Ran-GTP pulls SREBP into nucleus
questions.explain: When cholesterol is low, the cholesterol-binding site on SCAP is empty, causing SCAP to change conformation. This allows SCAP+SREBP to be packaged into transport vesicles and delivered to the Golgi, where site-1 and site-2 proteases cleave SREBP, releasing its cytosolic domain to enter the nucleus. This is anchoring + proteolytic release, not phosphorylation.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the state of NF-AT in a RESTING (non-activated) T cell?
options: NF-AT is dephosphorylated, NLS exposed, and concentrated in the nucleus
NF-AT is phosphorylated, NES exposed, and retained in the cytoplasm
NF-AT is phosphorylated, NLS exposed, and imported into the nucleus
NF-AT is dephosphorylated, NES exposed, and exported to the cytoplasm
NF-AT is unphosphorylated and bound to calcineurin in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: In resting T cells, NF-AT is phosphorylated, which masks the NLS and exposes the NES, retaining NF-AT in the cytoplasm. Upon T-cell activation, Ca²⁺ rises and activates calcineurin, which dephosphorylates NF-AT, exposing the NLS and masking the NES, driving nuclear import.

questions.q: Cyclosporin is an immunosuppressive drug used to prevent organ transplant rejection. What is its mechanism of action in the context of nuclear transport?
options: Cyclosporin inhibits Ran-GEF, preventing Ran-GTP accumulation in the nucleus
Cyclosporin blocks importins from binding FG repeats in the NPC
Cyclosporin inhibits calcineurin, preventing dephosphorylation of NF-AT and keeping T cells inactive
Cyclosporin promotes SCAP-mediated retention of NF-AT in the ER
Cyclosporin degrades exportins, trapping NF-AT permanently in the nucleus
questions.explain: Cyclosporin inhibits calcineurin (the protein phosphatase activated by Ca²⁺ in T cells). Without calcineurin, NF-AT remains phosphorylated and cytoplasmic, so it cannot enter the nucleus to activate T-cell genes. This suppresses the immune response, preventing rejection of transplanted organs.

questions.q: In the NF-AT system, phosphorylation of NF-AT leads to which outcome?
options: Enhanced nuclear import because importins recognize phosphorylated NLS
Cytoplasmic retention because the NES is exposed and the NLS is masked
Increased calcineurin activity, creating a positive feedback loop
Direct binding to Ran-GTP, which exports NF-AT from the nucleus
Proteolytic release of the NF-AT nuclear domain from the plasma membrane
questions.explain: Phosphorylation of NF-AT masks the NLS and exposes the NES, resulting in cytoplasmic retention. This is a key exam trap — students may assume phosphorylation promotes import (as in some other signalling contexts), but here phosphorylation promotes cytoplasmic retention and export.

questions.q: In the FRET-based Ran-GTP probe (YFP–RBD–CFP), what is observed when Ran-GTP is ABSENT?
options: The probe is in an open conformation, YFP and CFP are far apart, and no FRET occurs
The probe is in a compact conformation, YFP and CFP are close together, and FRET occurs
Ran-GDP binds RBD and separates the two fluorophores, abolishing FRET
CFP is excited but emits no signal because YFP quenches it at long range
FRET occurs only when both Ran-GTP and Ran-GDP are simultaneously present
questions.explain: Without Ran-GTP, the RBD adopts a compact conformation that keeps YFP and CFP close together, so FRET occurs (energy transfers from CFP to YFP). When Ran-GTP binds RBD, the conformation opens, separating the fluorophores and abolishing FRET. A LOW FRET ratio therefore indicates HIGH Ran-GTP.

questions.q: What does a LOW I_FRET/I_CFP ratio in the Ran-GTP FRET probe experiment indicate?
options: Low Ran-GTP concentration — the probe is closed and FRET is occurring
High Ran-GTP concentration — the probe is open and FRET is not occurring
High Ran-GDP concentration — RBD conformation is extended
Equal Ran-GTP and Ran-GDP — the probe is in an intermediate state
The probe is malfunctioning — a low ratio always indicates probe degradation
questions.explain: A low I_FRET/I_CFP ratio means that YFP emission is low relative to CFP emission — i.e., no FRET is occurring. This happens when Ran-GTP binds RBD and opens the probe, separating CFP and YFP. Therefore, a low ratio corresponds to HIGH Ran-GTP. This inverse relationship is the most common exam trap for this topic.

questions.q: In interphase cells, where is Ran-GTP enriched according to the FRET probe experiment using Xenopus sperm nuclei?
options: In the cytoplasm near the plasma membrane
Equally distributed between nucleus and cytoplasm
Inside the nucleus
At the surface of the NPC on the cytoplasmic face
At the centrosome, to aid in spindle assembly during interphase
questions.explain: The FRET probe showed the lowest I_FRET/I_CFP ratio (highest Ran-GTP) inside the nucleus during interphase, confirming that Ran-GEF in the nucleus generates a high Ran-GTP zone there. The control experiment using RanT24N (a Ran-GEF inhibitor) abolished this nuclear enrichment.

questions.q: During mitosis, where was the Ran-GTP FRET probe signal (lowest I_FRET/I_CFP) concentrated?
options: Inside the reformed nuclear envelope
At the kinetochores of condensed chromosomes only
Around the central spindle/chromosomes in mitotic egg extracts
Uniformly throughout the mitotic cytoplasm
At the cortex of the dividing cell near the cleavage furrow
questions.explain: During mitosis in Xenopus egg extracts, the FRET probe showed the lowest ratio (highest Ran-GTP) around the central spindle region surrounding chromosomes, suggesting Ran-GTP plays roles beyond nuclear transport — including spindle assembly — during cell division.

questions.q: Which of the following comparisons between NLS and ER signal sequences is CORRECT?
options: Both NLS and ER signal sequences are positively charged and are cleaved after targeting
NLS is positively charged and is NOT cleaved; ER signal sequence is hydrophobic and IS cleaved
NLS is hydrophobic and is cleaved; ER signal sequence is positively charged and is retained
Both NLS and ER signal sequences are retained on the protein after targeting to their respective organelle
NLS is cleaved in the nucleus; ER signal sequence is retained in the ER membrane
questions.explain: NLS sequences are short stretches of positively charged amino acids (Lys, Arg) that are NOT cleaved after nuclear import — they remain permanently on the protein. In contrast, ER signal sequences are hydrophobic and are co-translationally cleaved by signal peptidase in the ER lumen. Confusing these two is a classic exam mistake.

lectures.title: Lecture 12: Vesicular Traffic, Secretion and Endocytosis I

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the function of transport vesicles in eukaryotic cells?
options: They replicate DNA and distribute it to daughter cells during cell division
They carry specific cargo proteins between membrane-enclosed compartments
They generate ATP by oxidative phosphorylation at the inner mitochondrial membrane
They degrade misfolded proteins via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway
They synthesize secretory proteins on cytosolic ribosomes and release them directly
questions.explain: Transport vesicles are small, membrane-enclosed spherical structures enriched with specific cargo proteins destined for a target compartment. They mediate both the secretory and endocytic pathways. DNA replication, ATP synthesis, proteasomal degradation, and direct cytosolic protein release are all unrelated to vesicular transport.

questions.q: George Palade established the canonical secretory pathway. In what order does a newly synthesized secretory protein travel?
options: Golgi → ER → Plasma membrane
ER → Lysosome → Plasma membrane
ER → Golgi → Plasma membrane
Plasma membrane → Early endosome → ER
Golgi → Plasma membrane → Early endosome
questions.explain: Palade demonstrated that secretory proteins enter the ER lumen, travel through Golgi cisternae, and exit via secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane or extracellular space. The endocytic arm runs in the reverse direction (plasma membrane → endosome → lysosome). None of the other options reflect the correct anterograde secretory route.

questions.q: Novick and Schekman performed a yeast genetic screen using temperature-sensitive mutants. What phenotype distinguished secretory mutants from wild-type yeast in a density gradient centrifugation experiment?
options: Secretory mutants floated near the top because they secreted more vesicles into the medium
Secretory mutants sedimented more rapidly because they accumulated vesicles and organelles
Secretory mutants were indistinguishable from wild-type in density
Secretory mutants formed larger colonies because protein synthesis was upregulated
Secretory mutants had lower density due to reduced membrane lipid content
questions.explain: At the restrictive temperature, secretory mutants accumulated secretory proteins at defined steps (ER, Golgi, or vesicles), making the cells denser with accumulated organelles. This allowed them to be separated from wild-type cells by density gradient centrifugation. The mutants are denser — not lighter — because they accumulate vesicles rather than secreting them.

questions.q: In the Novick–Schekman yeast secretory mutant screen, mutant class C accumulated secretory proteins specifically in transport vesicles. What does this indicate about the function of the SEC genes mutated in class C?
options: The SEC genes in class C encode proteins required for cargo entry into the ER
The SEC genes in class C encode proteins required for vesicle budding from the ER
The SEC genes in class C encode proteins required for vesicle processing in the Golgi
The SEC genes in class C encode proteins required for vesicle fusion with the plasma membrane
The SEC genes in class C encode proteins required for retrograde transport from Golgi to ER
questions.explain: Cargo accumulating in transport vesicles means the vesicles form and bud successfully but cannot fuse with the plasma membrane. Therefore the mutated SEC genes in class C are required for the final fusion step. Class A blocks at ER exit and class B blocks at Golgi exit — not at vesicle fusion.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly pairs a coat protein type with its direction and route of transport?
options: COPI — anterograde — ER to Golgi
COPII — retrograde — Golgi to ER
Clathrin — anterograde — ER to Golgi
COPII — anterograde — ER to Golgi
COPI — anterograde — Golgi to plasma membrane
questions.explain: COPII mediates anterograde (forward) transport from the ER to the Golgi. COPI mediates retrograde (backward) transport from the Golgi back to the ER, as well as intra-Golgi transport. Clathrin mediates endocytosis from the plasma membrane and Golgi-to-endosome trafficking — not ER-to-Golgi transport.

questions.q: How do coat proteins physically generate membrane curvature during vesicle budding?
options: They insert hydrophobic domains into the inner leaflet of the lipid bilayer, stretching it outward
They polymerize into concave-shaped cage structures on the cytosolic face that force the bilayer to curve
They activate lipid flippases that transfer phospholipids from the inner to the outer leaflet
They recruit dynamin to the flat membrane, which then reshapes the membrane with its GTPase activity
They sever existing vesicles and reattach them to the donor membrane in a curved configuration
questions.explain: Coat proteins polymerize on the cytosolic face of donor membranes into concave-shaped cage structures that physically force the lipid bilayer to deform into a curved bud. Dynamin is specifically responsible for the scission (pinching-off) step, not for generating the initial curvature. Lipid flippases and hydrophobic insertions are not the primary mechanism described for coat-protein-mediated curvature.

questions.q: During clathrin-coated vesicle assembly, what is the role of adaptin?
options: It polymerizes into a cage lattice that physically deforms the membrane into a pit
It hydrolyzes GTP to pinch the coated pit off the plasma membrane
It links the cargo receptor on the donor membrane to the clathrin coat
It forms the transmembrane channel through which cargo proteins enter the vesicle
It anchors the completed vesicle to microtubule tracks for directed transport
questions.explain: Adaptin (adaptor protein) serves as the molecular bridge between the cargo receptor and clathrin. The assembly order is: cargo → cargo receptor → adaptin → clathrin. Clathrin itself forms the cage; dynamin performs GTP hydrolysis for scission; motor proteins handle microtubule-based transport.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the complete sequence of clathrin-coated vesicle formation from the plasma membrane?
options: Clathrin assembly → cargo receptor recruitment → adaptin binding → dynamin scission → coat retention on vesicle
Cargo recognition by cargo receptor → adaptin recruitment → clathrin polymerization → coated pit → dynamin scission → uncoating
Dynamin helix formation → clathrin cage assembly → cargo selection → adaptin linking → coated pit pinching
Cargo receptor recruitment → dynamin recruitment → clathrin assembly → adaptin linking → membrane deformation → fusion
Adaptin assembly → clathrin cage → cargo selection → GTP hydrolysis by clathrin → uncoating → naked vesicle
questions.explain: The correct sequence is: (1) cargo recognized by cargo receptor, (2) cargo receptor recruits adaptin, (3) adaptin recruits clathrin, (4) clathrin polymerizes into a coated pit, (5) dynamin performs GTP-dependent scission, (6) coat proteins dissociate (uncoating), yielding a naked transport vesicle. Dynamin acts after coat assembly, not before it.

questions.q: Dynamin is a GTPase that acts during vesicle budding. What is the specific mechanical role of dynamin?
options: It polymerizes into a cage around the budding vesicle to generate membrane curvature
It recognizes and clusters cargo molecules into the forming vesicle bud
It forms a helical ring around the membrane neck and uses GTP hydrolysis to sever it
It recruits cargo receptors to the cytosolic face of the donor membrane
It catalyzes SNARE complex assembly to fuse the newly budded vesicle with its target
questions.explain: Dynamin forms a helix around the neck of the clathrin-coated pit; GTP hydrolysis drives a conformational change that constricts and severs the neck, releasing the vesicle. Membrane curvature is generated by coat proteins, not dynamin. Cargo recruitment involves cargo receptors and adaptins, and SNARE assembly is a later docking/fusion event.

questions.q: Temperature-sensitive dynamin mutations in Drosophila cause flies to become paralyzed at high temperature. What is the cellular mechanism underlying this paralysis?
options: Dynamin is required for axonal microtubule assembly; its loss causes cytoskeletal collapse in motor neurons
Dynamin mediates retrograde axonal transport; its loss causes toxic cargo accumulation in synapses
Dynamin is needed for coat protein disassembly; its loss traps coat proteins and blocks all vesicular traffic
Dynamin is required for synaptic vesicle recycling; its loss depletes synaptic vesicles and blocks neurotransmission
Dynamin is required for SNARE complex disassembly; its loss prevents new rounds of vesicle fusion
questions.explain: Without functional dynamin, clathrin-coated pits accumulate at the plasma membrane but fail to pinch off. This means synaptic vesicles cannot be recycled after neurotransmitter release, depleting the vesicle pool and blocking synaptic transmission. The flies are paralyzed because nerve-muscle communication fails — not due to cytoskeletal collapse or SNARE dysfunction.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of coat proteins during vesicle budding?
options: Deforming the donor membrane into a curved bud shape
Concentrating specific cargo molecules into the forming vesicle
Recognizing cargo receptors on the donor membrane surface
Performing GTP hydrolysis to sever the vesicle neck from the donor membrane
Polymerizing on the cytosolic face of the membrane
questions.explain: GTP hydrolysis for neck scission is performed by dynamin, not by coat proteins. Coat proteins (COPI, COPII, clathrin) are responsible for membrane deformation, cargo concentration, and interaction with cargo receptors. Confusing dynamin's role with that of coat proteins is a common exam error.

questions.q: Which of the following problems in intracellular protein trafficking is solved by the endocytic transport pathway?
options: Delivering nuclear transcription factors from the cytoplasm into the nucleus
Transporting newly synthesized secretory proteins from the ER to the Golgi
Importing extracellular molecules such as nutrients and cholesterol into the cell
Recycling COPI coat proteins from Golgi vesicles back to the cytosol
Packaging digestive enzymes into zymogen granules for storage
questions.explain: The endocytic pathway solves the problem of bringing extracellular molecules (nutrients, cholesterol, ions) into the cell. Nuclear protein import is mediated by the nuclear pore complex, not endocytosis. ER-to-Golgi transport is handled by the secretory pathway (COPII), and zymogen granule packaging is also part of the secretory pathway.

questions.q: A researcher discovers a drug that blocks the uncoating step of vesicular transport (coat protein removal from vesicles). What would be the most likely direct consequence?
options: Vesicles would bud normally but would fail to dock and fuse with their target membrane
Coat proteins would be depleted from the cytosol, preventing new rounds of vesicle budding
Cargo would be mispackaged into wrong vesicle types
Dynamin would fail to sever vesicle necks because coat proteins are required for dynamin recruitment
v-SNAREs would be permanently sequestered in the coat, preventing SNARE-mediated fusion
questions.explain: Uncoating occurs after dynamin severs the vesicle neck. The coat must be shed before the vesicle can dock and fuse with the target. If uncoating is blocked, the coat-covered vesicle cannot interact properly with tethering factors and SNAREs on the target membrane, preventing fusion and cargo delivery. Coat protein depletion would be a secondary consequence, not the most direct effect.

questions.q: Vesicles move from donor to acceptor compartments along intracellular tracks. What provides these tracks, and what proteins do the walking?
options: Actin filaments serve as tracks; Rab GTPases walk along them using GTP hydrolysis
Microtubules serve as tracks; motor proteins walk along them to deliver vesicles directionally
Intermediate filaments serve as tracks; SNARE proteins drive vesicle movement using ATP
Microtubules serve as tracks; dynamin walks along them using GTP hydrolysis
Lipid bilayer continuities serve as tunnels; cargo proteins diffuse passively between compartments
questions.explain: Microtubules serve as the intracellular highways for vesicular transport, and motor proteins (kinesins and dyneins) on the vesicle surface walk along these tracks to deliver vesicles directionally to the target compartment. Rab GTPases mediate tethering at the target, not movement. Dynamin performs scission, not vesicle transport.

questions.q: What is the primary role of Rab proteins in vesicular transport?
options: Catalyzing GTP hydrolysis to power membrane fusion between vesicle and target
Polymerizing on the donor membrane to generate the vesicle coat
Marking vesicle/compartment identity and recruiting tethering proteins to initiate vesicle capture
Directly zippering v-SNARE and t-SNARE proteins together to fuse membranes
Severing vesicle necks from donor membranes during the budding reaction
questions.explain: Rab proteins are small GTPases on the vesicle surface that mark the identity of specific vesicles and compartments. Active (GTP-bound) Rab recruits tethering proteins on the target membrane to initiate the capture of the vesicle. SNAREs, not Rabs, perform the actual membrane fusion. Dynamin, not Rab, severs vesicle necks.

questions.q: Tethering proteins reside on the target membrane. What are their two key functions in vesicular transport?
options: Generating membrane curvature and recruiting dynamin for scission
Hydrolyzing GTP to activate Rab proteins and depolymerizing the vesicle coat
Initially capturing the vesicle from a distance and assembling SNARE complexes
Recognizing cargo molecules on the vesicle and sorting them to the correct target
Polymerizing into a cage around the docked vesicle to prevent premature fusion
questions.explain: Tethering proteins perform two key functions: (1) they interact with Rab-GTP on the incoming vesicle to initially capture it from a distance, and (2) they help assemble SNARE complexes to facilitate bilayer fusion. They do not generate curvature (coat proteins), hydrolyze GTP for scission (dynamin), or form cages around docked vesicles.

questions.q: How do SNAREs drive membrane fusion between a transport vesicle and its target compartment?
options: v-SNARE on the vesicle and t-SNARE on the target zipper together, pulling the two membranes into close contact until they fuse
SNAREs hydrolyze ATP to power a mechanical piston that forces the two membranes together
SNAREs recruit phospholipases that digest membrane lipids, enabling spontaneous bilayer mixing
v-SNARE and t-SNARE form a pore channel through which cargo proteins diffuse without membrane fusion
SNAREs activate Rab GTPase, which then recruits coat proteins to fuse the membranes
questions.explain: Complementary v-SNARE (on the vesicle) and t-SNARE (on the target membrane) intertwine in a zippering reaction that pulls the two bilayers into extremely close proximity, driving lipid bilayer fusion and cargo release. SNAREs do not use ATP hydrolysis; that is characteristic of motor proteins. SNAREs also do not form pores or recruit coat proteins.

questions.q: A transport vesicle carrying v-SNARE type X approaches two potential target membranes: one bearing t-SNARE type X and one bearing t-SNARE type Y. Which membrane will the vesicle fuse with, and why?
options: The membrane bearing t-SNARE type Y, because mismatched SNAREs create stronger binding forces
Both membranes equally, because vesicle fusion depends on Rab proteins rather than SNARE identity
Neither membrane, because SNARE-mediated fusion requires coat proteins to remain on the vesicle
The membrane bearing t-SNARE type X, because complementary SNARE pairs are required for efficient fusion
The membrane bearing t-SNARE type Y, because tethering proteins recognize mismatched SNAREs
questions.explain: SNARE pairing is highly specific — v-SNARE type X on the vesicle efficiently interacts only with its matching t-SNARE type X on the target membrane, enabling fusion. This combinatorial SNARE code ensures vesicles fuse only with the correct target compartment. Mismatched SNARE pairs (type X + type Y) do not support efficient fusion.

questions.q: In the SNARE-mediated fusion sequence, which event occurs FIRST?
options: v-SNARE and t-SNARE zipper together to pull membranes close
Lipid bilayers merge and cargo is released into the target lumen
Rab-GTP on the vesicle recruits the tethering protein on the target membrane
Membranes contact and begin to coalesce
Tethering protein assembles the SNARE complex
questions.explain: The sequence begins with tethering: Rab-GTP on the vesicle surface contacts the tethering protein on the target membrane, capturing the vesicle from a distance. Only after tethering do the v-SNARE and t-SNARE begin to zipper together (docking), followed by membrane coalescence and finally complete bilayer fusion with cargo release.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why vesicles do not randomly fuse with any membrane in the cell?
options: Coat proteins remain on vesicles permanently, physically blocking access to non-target membranes
Only dynamin-coated membranes are competent to receive incoming vesicles
Rab identity, tethering factor complementarity, and compartment-specific SNARE pairing collectively ensure targeting specificity
Microtubule tracks are unidirectional and physically prevent vesicles from reaching wrong compartments
Cargo receptors on vesicles recognize surface markers on the correct target membrane, bypassing all SNARE interactions
questions.explain: Specificity of vesicle targeting arises from multiple layers: (1) Rab GTPases mark vesicle and compartment identity, (2) tethering factors on the target recognize the correct Rab, and (3) compartment-specific v-SNARE/t-SNARE pairs ensure only matching pairs fuse. This multi-step specificity prevents mistargeting. Coats are shed before fusion, and microtubule directionality alone cannot account for organelle-level targeting specificity.

questions.q: In the yeast autophagosome–vacuole fusion system, what is the role of Mon1-Ccz1?
options: It is a v-SNARE on the autophagosome membrane that pairs with vacuolar t-SNARE
It is the tethering complex that captures the autophagosome on the vacuole surface
It is a GEF (guanine nucleotide exchange factor) that activates the Rab GTPase Ypt7 on the autophagosome
It is a GTPase-activating protein that inactivates Ypt7 to terminate fusion
It is the coat protein complex that generates autophagosome membrane curvature
questions.explain: Mon1-Ccz1 is the guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) that is targeted to the autophagosome by Atg8 and activates Ypt7 (the Rab GTPase) by exchanging GDP for GTP. Ypt7-GTP then recruits the HOPS tethering complex on the vacuole. HOPS, not Mon1-Ccz1, is the tethering complex. Ykt6 is the v-SNARE on the autophagosome.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about COPI-coated vesicles is correct?
options: COPI mediates anterograde transport from the ER to the Golgi
COPI mediates retrograde transport from the Golgi back to the ER and intra-Golgi transport
COPI mediates endocytosis from the plasma membrane to early endosomes
COPI mediates transport from the Golgi to late endosomes
COPI mediates anterograde transport from the Golgi to the plasma membrane
questions.explain: COPI coat mediates retrograde transport (Golgi → ER) and intra-Golgi transport. COPII handles the anterograde ER-to-Golgi direction. Clathrin handles endocytosis from the plasma membrane and Golgi-to-endosome transport. Mixing up COPI and COPII directions is among the most common exam errors.

questions.q: A cell biologist uses electron microscopy to examine a dynamin temperature-sensitive mutant at the restrictive temperature. What would she observe at the plasma membrane?
options: Abundant naked transport vesicles that have been shed but cannot be re-coated
Clathrin cages forming in the cytosol independently of the membrane
Clathrin-coated pits that extend as long membrane tubules but fail to pinch off
Complete absence of clathrin structures because uncoating is blocked
Fully formed uncoated vesicles accumulating beneath the plasma membrane
questions.explain: When dynamin is non-functional, clathrin-coated pits form and invaginate normally (coat assembly is intact), but they cannot be severed from the plasma membrane. The pits remain attached, forming long membrane tubules with attached pit structures visible by EM. This is the classic dynamin mutant phenotype observed in Drosophila temperature-sensitive experiments.

questions.q: What is the key distinction between the roles of Rab proteins/tethering factors and SNARE proteins in vesicular transport?
options: Rab proteins and tethering factors fuse membranes; SNAREs only mediate cargo recognition
Rab proteins coat the vesicle; tethering factors coat the target; SNAREs uncoat the vesicle after fusion
Rab proteins and tethering factors mediate initial vesicle targeting and capture; SNAREs catalyze the actual lipid bilayer fusion
Rab proteins and SNAREs together drive budding; tethering factors drive coat assembly
Rab proteins and tethering factors hydrolyze GTP for membrane fusion; SNAREs stabilize the coat during budding
questions.explain: Rab proteins (small GTPases) mark vesicle identity and recruit tethering factors, which capture the incoming vesicle at the target membrane — these steps constitute targeting. SNAREs are the actual fusion machinery: v-SNARE/t-SNARE zippering drives lipid bilayer merger. Confusing targeting (Rab/tethering) with fusion (SNARE) is a classic exam trap.

questions.q: According to the Bonifacino and Glick 7-step cycle, which step immediately follows dynamin-mediated scission?
options: Initiation — cargo and v-SNARE associate with coat proteins at the donor membrane
Tethering — Rab-GTP on the vesicle contacts the tethering factor on the target membrane
Docking — v-SNARE and t-SNARE zipper together
Uncoating — coat proteins dissociate from the vesicle back to the cytosol
Fusion — lipid bilayers merge and cargo is released
questions.explain: The 7-step cycle is: (1) initiation, (2) budding, (3) scission (dynamin), (4) uncoating, (5) tethering, (6) docking, (7) fusion. Immediately after scission, the coat must be shed (uncoating) so that the vesicle can interact with tethering factors and SNAREs. Tethering and docking occur only after uncoating.

questions.q: Which Nobel Prize is correctly matched to both the scientist and the discovery?
options: 1974 Nobel — Randy Schekman — yeast genetic screen identifying SEC genes
2013 Nobel — George Palade — establishment of the secretory pathway by EM autoradiography
2013 Nobel — James Rothman — discovery of the SNARE mechanism of vesicle fusion
1974 Nobel — James Rothman — discovery of clathrin-coated vesicles by electron microscopy
2013 Nobel — Randy Schekman — discovery of dynamin and its role in vesicle scission
questions.explain: James Rothman shared the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovering the SNARE mechanism of vesicle fusion. George Palade received the 1974 Nobel for establishing the secretory pathway. Randy Schekman shared the 2013 Nobel for the yeast genetic screen identifying SEC genes — not for dynamin. Dynamin is not a Nobel-recognized discovery covered in this lecture.

questions.q: A mutant cell expresses a v-SNARE that cannot interact with any t-SNARE. Which step in the 7-step vesicular transport cycle would be most directly blocked?
options: Step 1 (initiation) — cargo would not associate with coat proteins
Step 3 (scission) — dynamin would not be recruited to the budding vesicle
Step 4 (uncoating) — coat proteins would remain permanently on the vesicle
Step 5 (tethering) — Rab-GTP would not recruit tethering factors
Step 6 (docking) — v-SNARE and t-SNARE could not zipper together
questions.explain: v-SNARE/t-SNARE interaction is specifically required for step 6 (docking), where the two SNARE proteins zipper together to pull the membranes into contact before fusion. Budding, scission, uncoating, and tethering would proceed normally because those steps involve coat proteins, dynamin, and Rab/tethering factors — none of which require v-SNARE/t-SNARE pairing.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three essential steps of every vesicular transport event?
options: Vesicle budding and cargo packaging at the donor membrane
Vesicle transport along microtubule tracks to the acceptor compartment
Uncoating of the vesicle to remove coat proteins after scission
Vesicle fusion with the target organelle to release cargo
All three of the other options are essential steps
questions.explain: The three essential steps described in the lecture are: (1) vesicle budding and cargo packaging, (2) vesicle transport along microtubules, and (3) vesicle fusion with the target organelle. Uncoating is a substep within the broader cycle but is not listed as one of the three 'essential steps' defined in this lecture. Options A, B, and D correspond to the three essential steps.

questions.q: In the autophagosome–vacuole fusion cascade, which molecule directly recruits the HOPS tethering complex to the target vacuole?
options: Atg8, by directly binding HOPS on the autophagosome surface
Mon1-Ccz1, by acting as the tethering scaffold bridging the two membranes
Ykt6, the v-SNARE, by engaging t-SNAREs on the vacuole surface
Ypt7-GTP, the activated Rab GTPase on the autophagosome, by binding HOPS on the vacuole
The COPII coat, which is retained on the autophagosome and recruits HOPS
questions.explain: The sequence is: Atg8 recruits Mon1-Ccz1 (GEF) to the autophagosome → Mon1-Ccz1 activates Ypt7 by GTP loading → Ypt7-GTP on the autophagosome then binds HOPS on the vacuole → HOPS assembles the SNARE complex (including Ykt6 v-SNARE) → fusion. HOPS is recruited by Ypt7-GTP, not directly by Atg8 or Mon1-Ccz1.

questions.q: A researcher inhibits clathrin assembly specifically. Which of the following trafficking routes would be most directly disrupted?
options: Retrograde ER retrieval of escaped ER-resident proteins (Golgi → ER)
Anterograde trafficking of newly synthesized secretory proteins (ER → Golgi)
Endocytosis of receptor–ligand complexes from the plasma membrane
Intra-Golgi cisternal maturation
Protein import into the nucleus through the nuclear pore complex
questions.explain: Clathrin is the coat protein responsible for endocytosis from the plasma membrane (as well as Golgi-to-endosome transport). Inhibiting clathrin assembly would most directly disrupt receptor-mediated endocytosis. Retrograde Golgi-to-ER transport uses COPI; ER-to-Golgi transport uses COPII. Nuclear import uses the nuclear pore complex, not coat proteins.

lectures.title: Lecture 13: Vesicular Traffic, Secretion and Endocytosis II

questions.q: Which coat protein mediates anterograde transport of cargo from the ER to the Golgi apparatus?
options: COPII
COPI
Clathrin
Dynamin
Sar1
questions.explain: COPII coat assembles on the ER membrane and selects cargo for anterograde (forward) transport to the Golgi. COPI is responsible for retrograde transport from the Golgi back to the ER and for intra-Golgi transport, making it a common source of confusion with COPII.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the role of dynamin in vesicular transport?
options: It selects transmembrane cargo proteins for packaging into vesicles
It mediates membrane fusion between vesicle and target membrane
It acts as a GTPase to pinch off (scission) vesicles from the donor membrane
It tethers vesicles to the correct acceptor compartment
It forms the outer scaffold layer of COPII vesicles
questions.explain: Dynamin is a GTPase that wraps around the neck of a budding vesicle and uses GTP hydrolysis to drive membrane scission, releasing the vesicle. Membrane fusion is mediated by SNARE proteins, not dynamin, which is a common point of confusion.

questions.q: Which pair of proteins constitutes the inner layer of the COPII coat that directly recognises transmembrane cargo?
options: Sec13 and Sec31
Sar1 and Sec24
Sec23 and Sec24
Rab1 and tethering factor
v-SNARE and t-SNARE
questions.explain: Sec23/24 forms the inner layer of the COPII coat; Sec24 directly recognises cytoplasmic tail signals on transmembrane cargo proteins. Sec13/31 forms the outer scaffold layer. Sar1 is the GTPase that initiates coat assembly but is not classified as the inner cargo-recognition layer.

questions.q: What is the immediate consequence of Sar1 GTP hydrolysis after a COPII vesicle has budded?
options: Sar1 recruits additional Sec23/24 complexes to the vesicle
The vesicle fuses with the Golgi membrane
The COPII coat disassembles (uncoating)
Dynamin wraps around the vesicle neck and pinches it off
The KDEL receptor is activated to retrieve escaped proteins
questions.explain: Sar1 GTP hydrolysis triggers disassembly of the COPII coat, allowing the vesicle to become competent for membrane fusion with its target. Students commonly think GTP hydrolysis drives budding, but it actually drives uncoating after scission.

questions.q: If a non-hydrolysable GTP analogue is added to a cell, what would most likely happen to COPII vesicles?
options: They would fail to bud from the ER membrane
They would bud normally but remain coated and fail to fuse with the target compartment
They would deliver cargo to the lysosome instead of the Golgi
Sar1 would fail to insert into the ER membrane
Sec24 would lose its ability to bind cargo
questions.explain: Non-hydrolysable GTP prevents Sar1 from completing its GTPase cycle, so the coat cannot disassemble. Vesicles form and bud normally but remain trapped as coated vesicles unable to uncoat and fuse. This parallels the GTPγS effect seen in Rothman's Golgi transport assay.

questions.q: Where do COPII vesicles primarily deliver their cargo after budding from the ER?
options: Directly to the trans-Golgi network
To the vesicular tubular cluster (VTC/ERGIC)
To the late endosome
Directly to the plasma membrane
To the recycling endosome
questions.explain: COPII vesicles travel to the vesicular tubular cluster (VTC), also called the ER-Golgi intermediate compartment (ERGIC) in mammalian cells, which acts as an intermediate station between the ER and the Golgi. Delivery directly to the TGN would bypass the intervening Golgi cisternae.

questions.q: In what order does cargo flow through the Golgi cisternae?
options: Trans → Medial → Cis → TGN
Cis → Trans → Medial → TGN
Cis → Medial → Trans → TGN
TGN → Trans → Medial → Cis
Medial → Cis → Trans → TGN
questions.explain: The Golgi has distinct polarity: cargo enters from the cis face (closest to ER), moves through medial cisternae, then reaches the trans cisterna, and finally exits via the TGN. Each cisterna contains distinct glycosylation enzymes that act sequentially on the cargo.

questions.q: Which sequence serves as the retrieval signal for soluble ER-resident proteins that escape to the Golgi?
options: Mannose-6-phosphate
KDEL
Di-leucine motif
Signal peptide
GPI anchor
questions.explain: KDEL is the C-terminal tetrapeptide motif on soluble ER-resident proteins such as BiP and PDI. If these proteins escape to the Golgi, the KDEL receptor recognises the KDEL sequence and loads them into COPI vesicles for retrograde retrieval. Mannose-6-phosphate is the signal for lysosomal targeting, not ER retention.

questions.q: In Rothman's in vitro Golgi transport assay, which experimental condition gives approximately 420 cpm of ³H-GlcNAc incorporation?
options: Complete reaction (positive control)
Reaction lacking ATP
Reaction lacking cytosol
Reaction with added GTPγS (non-hydrolysable GTP)
Reaction lacking Golgi membranes
questions.explain: GTPγS addition gives ~420 cpm compared to 3500 cpm in the complete reaction. GTPγS traps GTPase-dependent coat proteins in the GTP-bound state, blocking coat disassembly and therefore preventing vesicle fusion. This result confirmed that GTP hydrolysis is required for vesicle uncoating/fusion, not budding.

questions.q: Why does removing ATP from Rothman's Golgi transport assay reduce ³H-GlcNAc incorporation to ~50 cpm?
options: ATP is required for KDEL receptor binding to cargo
ATP is needed for coat protein synthesis in vitro
ATP provides energy for vesicle budding, membrane fusion, and coat protein dynamics
ATP activates the KDEL retrieval receptor only
ATP is the direct substrate for dynamin's pinching activity
questions.explain: Vesicular transport requires multiple ATP-dependent steps including coat assembly, vesicle budding, and SNARE-mediated fusion events. Without ATP, essentially no transport occurs (50 cpm vs 3500 cpm complete). ATP is not specific to one step — it is broadly required throughout the transport cycle.

questions.q: How does the HIV protein Nef cause immune evasion?
options: It prevents MHC-I synthesis in the ER
It blocks COPII export of MHC-I from the ER
It reroutes MHC-I from the TGN to the lysosome for degradation
It inhibits dynamin so MHC-I cannot be endocytosed
It displaces the KDEL receptor so MHC-I is retrieved back to the ER
questions.explain: HIV Nef associates with MHC-I at the TGN/Golgi and hijacks TGN sorting machinery to divert MHC-I into vesicles destined for late endosomes and lysosomes, where MHC-I is degraded. Without MHC-I on the plasma membrane, cytotoxic T lymphocytes cannot recognise infected cells.

questions.q: Which statement about the KDEL retrieval mechanism is correct?
options: The KDEL receptor binds KDEL-tagged proteins with high affinity in the ER lumen (neutral pH) and releases them in the acidic Golgi
The KDEL receptor is a COPII component that directly deforms the ER membrane
The KDEL receptor binds KDEL in the acidic Golgi and releases KDEL-tagged protein in the neutral ER lumen
KDEL-tagged proteins are degraded in the lysosome rather than retrieved
KDEL is a transmembrane signal recognised by Sec24 in the ER
questions.explain: The KDEL receptor has higher affinity for KDEL in the slightly acidic Golgi lumen, allowing it to capture escaped ER-resident proteins. Once the COPI vesicle delivers the receptor-cargo complex back to the neutral ER lumen, the receptor releases the protein. Confusing which pH promotes binding vs. release is a classic exam error.

questions.q: Which destinations does the TGN dispatch cargo to? Select the most complete answer.
options: ER and cis-Golgi only
Lysosomes and secretory vesicles only
Late endosomes/lysosomes, secretory vesicles, plasma membrane, and recycling endosomes
Early endosome and recycling endosome only
Plasma membrane and ER only
questions.explain: The TGN is the major cargo-sorting hub of the cell, simultaneously dispatching vesicles to late endosomes/lysosomes (via mannose-6-phosphate/clathrin), secretory vesicles for exocytosis, the plasma membrane, and recycling endosomes. Limiting this to only two destinations misses the full scope of TGN function.

questions.q: In polarised intestinal epithelial cells, which surface faces the gut lumen and bears microvilli for nutrient import?
options: Basolateral surface
Apical surface
Basal lamina surface
Endosomal membrane
Trans-Golgi network membrane
questions.explain: The apical surface of polarised epithelial cells faces the gut lumen and is specialised for absorption via microvilli. The basolateral surface faces the basal lamina and blood vessels, and is responsible for exporting absorbed nutrients. TGN sorting ensures distinct membrane proteins reach their correct apical or basolateral domain.

questions.q: A researcher blocks the TGN sorting machinery in polarised epithelial cells. What is the most likely consequence?
options: All secretory proteins accumulate permanently in the ER
Cargo is mistargeted — proteins destined for the apical surface may appear at the basolateral surface or vice versa
COPII vesicles stop budding from the ER
The KDEL receptor cannot retrieve escaped ER-resident proteins
Lysosomal hydrolases are constitutively secreted into the cytosol
questions.explain: The TGN acts as the sorting hub that directs proteins to apical vs. basolateral membranes in polarised cells. Disrupting TGN sorting leads to mistargeting, where proteins designed for one domain end up at the wrong membrane. ER accumulation would result from blocking ER export (COPII), not TGN sorting.

questions.q: How does MHC-I normally travel to display viral peptides to cytotoxic T lymphocytes?
options: MHC-I is synthesised in the cytosol and directly inserted into the plasma membrane
MHC-I travels ER → Golgi → TGN → plasma membrane via the secretory pathway
MHC-I is synthesised in the nucleus and exported via nuclear pores
MHC-I is delivered from the lysosome to the plasma membrane via exocytosis
MHC-I bypasses the Golgi and travels directly from the ER to the plasma membrane
questions.explain: MHC-I follows the canonical secretory pathway: ER → Golgi → TGN → plasma membrane. At the plasma membrane it presents viral peptides to cytotoxic T lymphocytes. HIV Nef hijacks the TGN step to reroute MHC-I to the lysosome instead, preventing immune recognition.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a major function of endocytosis?
options: Uptake of extracellular nutrients
Recycling of plasma membrane lipids
Synthesis of new membrane proteins in the ER
Cellular signalling via internalised receptors
Immune defence through phagocytosis
questions.explain: Membrane protein synthesis occurs in the ER as part of the secretory pathway — this is independent of endocytosis. Endocytosis serves nutrient uptake, membrane recycling, signalling, cell adhesion/migration, and immune defence. This is an exception/NOT question testing knowledge of endocytosis boundaries.

questions.q: Which type of endocytosis is restricted to specialised immune cells such as macrophages and neutrophils?
options: Pinocytosis
Clathrin-mediated endocytosis
Phagocytosis
Receptor-mediated endocytosis
Macropinocytosis
questions.explain: Phagocytosis involves pseudopod extension around large particles (~5 μm) such as bacteria or dead cells and is performed only by specialised phagocytic cells like macrophages and neutrophils. Pinocytosis and clathrin-mediated endocytosis occur in virtually all cell types.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of compartments through which internalised cargo travels during the endocytic pathway?
options: Plasma membrane → Late endosome → Early endosome → Lysosome
Plasma membrane → Early endosome → Recycling endosome → Lysosome
Plasma membrane → Clathrin-coated vesicle → Early endosome → Late endosome → Lysosome
Plasma membrane → Clathrin-coated vesicle → Lysosome → Late endosome
Plasma membrane → Early endosome → Lysosome → Late endosome
questions.explain: The correct sequence is: clathrin-coated pit → clathrin-coated vesicle → uncoating → early endosome → late endosome (multivesicular body) → lysosome. The early endosome also sends receptors back to the plasma membrane via the recycling endosome, but cargo degradation follows the forward route to the lysosome.

questions.q: At which point in the endocytic pathway does receptor-ligand dissociation first begin, and why?
options: At the plasma membrane, due to competition from extracellular ligands
In the clathrin-coated vesicle, due to coat protein-induced conformational changes
In the early endosome, due to its slightly acidic lumen
In the late endosome, due to complete acidification to pH 4.5
In the lysosome, due to hydrolytic enzyme activity
questions.explain: The slightly acidic lumen of the early endosome triggers conformational changes that reduce receptor-ligand affinity, initiating dissociation. This allows receptors to be sorted back to the plasma membrane for recycling while ligands continue to the lysosome. The lysosome is where final hydrolytic degradation occurs, not initial dissociation.

questions.q: What is the outer shell of an LDL particle primarily composed of?
options: Cholesteryl esters and free cholesterol only
A phospholipid monolayer with apolipoprotein B on the surface
A phospholipid bilayer with mannose-6-phosphate receptors
A clathrin coat with dynamin at the neck
A glycoprotein lattice with KDEL-tagged proteins
questions.explain: The outer shell of LDL consists of a phospholipid monolayer (not bilayer) studded with apolipoprotein B, which is the ligand recognised by the LDL receptor. The core contains cholesteryl esters and free cholesterol. Confusing the monolayer with a bilayer is a common structural error.

questions.q: How many times can a single LDL receptor be recycled during receptor-mediated endocytosis of LDL?
options: Only once — it is degraded along with LDL in the lysosome
Approximately 5–10 times
Approximately 200 times
Indefinitely — it is never degraded
Approximately 50 times, until the clathrin coat wears out
questions.explain: The LDL receptor is recycled back to the plasma membrane approximately 200 times. This is a key advantage of receptor-mediated endocytosis — the receptor is reused, while the LDL cargo is delivered to the lysosome for degradation and cholesterol release.

questions.q: A drug prevents LDL from dissociating from its receptor in acidic conditions. What is the most likely outcome?
options: LDL is delivered to the lysosome faster and cytosolic cholesterol increases
The LDL receptor is recycled normally and cytosolic cholesterol is unaffected
The LDL receptor cannot be recycled; cytosolic cholesterol decreases
LDL is redirected to the early endosome via COPI vesicles
Clathrin-coated pit formation at the plasma membrane is inhibited
questions.explain: If LDL cannot dissociate from LDLR in the acidic early endosome, the receptor remains bound to LDL and cannot be sorted to the recycling endosome. The receptor stays on the endosomal membrane and is eventually degraded. Without functional receptor recycling, LDL uptake is impaired and cytosolic cholesterol decreases.

questions.q: Familial hypercholesterolaemia is caused by loss-of-function mutations in which gene?
options: Apolipoprotein B (APOB)
LDL receptor (LDLR)
Dynamin (DNM2)
Clathrin heavy chain (CLTC)
HMG-CoA reductase (HMGCR)
questions.explain: Familial hypercholesterolaemia results from loss-of-function mutations in the LDLR gene. Without functional LDL receptors, LDL cannot be cleared from the blood, causing 6–10-fold elevation in plasma LDL and early-onset atherosclerosis. HMGCR encodes the target of statins but its mutation does not cause this disease.

questions.q: Statins treat hypercholesterolaemia by which mechanism?
options: Blocking clathrin-mediated endocytosis of LDL
Increasing the number of LDL receptors on hepatocyte surfaces
Inhibiting a key enzyme in the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway
Promoting LDLR recycling from early endosomes
Directly binding LDL particles and preventing their uptake
questions.explain: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, a key enzyme in the mevalonate/cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, thereby reducing intracellular cholesterol production. This is distinct from improving receptor-mediated clearance; statins do not directly enhance endocytosis or receptor recycling.

questions.q: What are the two key advantages of receptor-mediated endocytosis compared to bulk-phase endocytosis?
options: Speed and temperature independence
Efficiency (captures low-concentration ligands) and selectivity (only receptor-recognised molecules internalised)
Large cargo capacity and independence from clathrin
Ability to function without ATP and without dynamin
Exclusive use by immune cells and recycling of all cargo
questions.explain: Receptor-mediated endocytosis provides efficiency — even molecules present at very low extracellular concentrations can be captured by concentrating them in coated pits — and selectivity, because only molecules recognised by specific receptor proteins are internalised. These properties are illustrated by the LDL-LDLR system.

questions.q: Which receptor does the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein bind to gain entry into host cells?
options: LDL receptor (LDLR)
KDEL receptor
ACE2 (angiotensin-converting enzyme 2)
Mannose-6-phosphate receptor
MHC-I on the host cell surface
questions.explain: SARS-CoV-2 uses its spike (S) glycoprotein to bind ACE2, a type I membrane protein expressed on lung, heart, kidney, and intestinal cells. ACE2 normally functions in blood pressure regulation. The virus enters via endocytosis and subsequent membrane fusion in the endosome.

questions.q: At which intracellular compartment does SARS-CoV-2 primarily assemble new virions?
options: Nucleus
ER-Golgi intermediate compartment (ERGIC)
Lysosome
Early endosome
Plasma membrane directly
questions.explain: SARS-CoV-2 virion assembly occurs at the ERGIC (ER-Golgi intermediate compartment), the same compartment that serves as the intermediate station between ER and Golgi in the secretory pathway. Assembled virions are then packaged into smooth-walled vesicles and released by exocytosis.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes how SARS-CoV-2 exploits the host cell's membrane trafficking machinery?
options: It uses COPI vesicles to export viral RNA from the nucleus to the cytosol
It hijacks the endocytic pathway for cell entry and the secretory pathway (ER, ERGIC, Golgi) for protein processing and assembly
It inserts its genome directly through the plasma membrane without using any vesicular compartments
It uses the lysosomal degradation pathway to process its own structural proteins
It exclusively uses clathrin-independent endocytosis pathways for cell entry
questions.explain: SARS-CoV-2 exploits the host endocytic pathway for cell entry (spike binds ACE2 → endocytosis → membrane fusion releases RNA) and the secretory pathway compartments (ER for structural protein synthesis, ERGIC for assembly, Golgi for glycosylation) for replication and virion production.

questions.q: COPI coat protein mediates which of the following transport steps? Select the most complete answer.
options: ER to Golgi (anterograde) and TGN to lysosome
Intra-Golgi transport only
Retrograde transport from Golgi to ER and intra-Golgi transport
Clathrin-coated vesicle formation at the plasma membrane
Recycling of LDL receptors from early endosomes to the plasma membrane
questions.explain: COPI mediates two distinct steps: (1) retrograde retrieval of escaped ER-resident proteins from the Golgi back to the ER, and (2) intra-Golgi transport between cisternae. COPII (not COPI) mediates anterograde ER-to-Golgi transport, and clathrin mediates endocytosis and TGN-to-lysosome trafficking.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about protein degradation in cells is FALSE?
options: It destroys misfolded and aggregated proteins before they harm the cell.
It is NOT important for regular protein turn-over.
It allows immune cells to digest invading bacteria via lysosomes.
It removes short-lived regulatory proteins such as cell cycle regulators.
It contributes to cellular quality control by clearing damaged proteins.
questions.explain: Protein degradation IS essential for regular protein turn-over — it replaces functional proteins that have reached the end of their lifespan. Option B is the false statement, as stated directly in the lecture exam trap. All other options correctly describe functions of protein degradation.

questions.q: Which enzyme has the SHORTEST half-life among those listed in the lecture?
options: Glucokinase (1.2 days)
Tyrosine aminotransferase (1.5 hours)
HMG CoA reductase (3 hours)
Ornithine decarboxylase (11 minutes)
δ-Aminolevulinate synthetase (70 minutes)
questions.explain: Ornithine decarboxylase has a half-life of just 11 minutes, making it the shortest-lived enzyme listed. This reflects its role as a tightly regulated metabolic enzyme. Lactic dehydrogenase, with 16 days, is the longest-lived.

questions.q: Lactic dehydrogenase has a half-life of 16 days. What category of protein would typically have a lifespan of seconds to minutes?
options: Structural proteins of bone
Metabolic enzymes in liver
Cell growth and division regulators
Muscle structural proteins
Extracellular matrix proteins
questions.explain: Cell growth and division regulators (such as cyclins) have extremely short half-lives — seconds to minutes — because rapid degradation allows precise control of the cell cycle. Structural proteins of bone and muscle can persist for months to years.

questions.q: Which protein aggregate is associated with Parkinson's disease, and what are the resulting inclusion bodies called?
options: Amyloid-β; senile plaques
TDP-43; stress granules
α-Synuclein; Lewy bodies
Tau; neurofibrillary tangles
Huntingtin; nuclear inclusions
questions.explain: Parkinson's disease is characterised by aggregation of α-Synuclein, forming Lewy bodies. Amyloid-β deposits are hallmarks of Alzheimer's, while TDP-43 inclusions spreading along motor pathways are characteristic of ALS.

questions.q: TDP-43 inclusions spreading along motor pathways are a hallmark lesion of which neurodegenerative disease?
options: Alzheimer's disease
Parkinson's disease
Huntington's disease
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
Multiple sclerosis
questions.explain: ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, 漸凍人症) is characterised by TDP-43 inclusions that spread along motor pathways, causing progressive motor neuron degeneration. Alzheimer's involves Aβ; Parkinson's involves α-Synuclein/Lewy bodies.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes the proteasome from the lysosome as sites of protein degradation?
options: The proteasome is found only in the nucleus; the lysosome is found only in the cytoplasm.
The proteasome degrades proteins, DNA, and lipids; the lysosome degrades only proteins.
The proteasome is a large protein complex in the nucleus and cytoplasm; the lysosome is an acidic organelle that also degrades bacteria and organelles.
The proteasome requires an acidic pH; the lysosome operates at neutral pH.
The proteasome can degrade entire organelles; the lysosome can only degrade small proteins.
questions.explain: The proteasome is a large protein complex found throughout the nucleus and cytoplasm, primarily degrading misfolded and short-lived proteins. The lysosome is an acidic organelle that degrades proteins, bacteria, damaged organelles, and viruses via multiple delivery pathways. Options A, D, and E reverse or misstate these properties.

questions.q: According to the protein quality control map, which of the following correctly describes the fate of a misfolded protein that is recognised by Hsc70?
options: It is ubiquitinated and sent to the proteasome via ERAD.
It forms a stress granule and awaits refolding.
It enters the secretory pathway and is exported via the Golgi.
It undergoes chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) directly into the lysosome.
It is packaged into an autophagosome via macroautophagy.
questions.explain: Misfolded proteins recognised by the Hsc70 chaperone are directed to the lysosome via chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), bypassing the formation of an autophagosome. Ubiquitinated misfolded proteins go to the proteasome (ERAD) or macroautophagy; Hsc70-recognised proteins uniquely use CMA.

questions.q: Ubiquitin is described as highly conserved. What percentage of sequence identity does human ubiquitin share with yeast ubiquitin?
questions.explain: Human and yeast ubiquitin share 96% sequence identity, reflecting the critical importance of its structure throughout eukaryotic evolution. Ubiquitin is 76 amino acids long — 76% is the amino acid count, not the identity figure.

questions.q: Ubiquitin is attached to target proteins via an isopeptide bond. Which specific amino acid on the target protein forms this bond?
options: Arginine
Glycine
Serine
Lysine
Cysteine
questions.explain: Ubiquitin's C-terminal glycine forms an isopeptide bond with a lysine residue on the target protein. This covalent attachment marks the protein for proteasomal or lysosomal degradation. Glycine is on ubiquitin itself, not on the substrate.

questions.q: Lys48-linked polyubiquitin chains direct a protein to the proteasome. What is the functional consequence of Lys63-linked polyubiquitin chains?
options: Direct degradation in the 20S proteasome core
Formation of stress granules
Routing to endocytic/lysosomal pathway
Activation of the E1 ubiquitin-activating enzyme
Nuclear export of the target protein
questions.explain: Lys63-linked polyubiquitin chains signal endocytic/lysosomal routing, not proteasomal degradation. This distinction is important because different chain linkages encode different cellular fates. Lys48-linked chains are the canonical proteasome-targeting signal.

questions.q: In the three-enzyme ubiquitination cascade, which enzyme provides substrate specificity by recognising degradation signals (degrons) on target proteins?
options: E1 — ubiquitin-activating enzyme
E2 — ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme
E3 — ubiquitin ligase
The 19S regulatory cap of the proteasome
The 20S core particle of the proteasome
questions.explain: E3 ubiquitin ligase recognises the specific degron (degradation signal) on each substrate protein, providing the substrate specificity of the ubiquitin-proteasome system. There are over 500 E3 ligases in humans for this reason. E1 activates ubiquitin using ATP; E2 carries the activated ubiquitin.

questions.q: Which enzyme in the ubiquitination cascade directly uses ATP hydrolysis to activate ubiquitin?
options: E3 ubiquitin ligase
E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme
E1 ubiquitin-activating enzyme
Deubiquitinase (DUB)
The 19S ATPase subunits
questions.explain: E1 uses ATP hydrolysis (ATP → AMP + PPi) to activate ubiquitin and transfer it to E2. E3 does not directly use ATP; the 19S ATPase subunits use ATP to unfold the substrate at the proteasome, which is a separate step downstream.

questions.q: Approximately how many E3 ubiquitin ligases are encoded in the human genome, and why does this number matter?
options: 2 — because one handles nuclear and one handles cytoplasmic substrates
~40 — because each handles a different cellular compartment
>500 — because the large number enables recognition of diverse substrate proteins
~10 — because each recognises a different amino acid degron
1 — because ubiquitin specificity is determined by the substrate itself
questions.explain: There are over 500 E3 ubiquitin ligases in humans, which explains how the ubiquitin-proteasome system can selectively target hundreds of different proteins. In contrast, only 2 E1 and ~40 E2 enzymes exist, acting as shared upstream activators.

questions.q: The 26S proteasome consists of a 20S core particle and a 19S regulatory cap. Which activity is performed by the 19S cap but NOT the 20S core?
options: Proteolytic cleavage of unfolded protein
Recognition of polyubiquitin chains and ATP-dependent unfolding
Formation of small peptide fragments from the target protein
Acidification of the interior chamber
Glycosylation of luminal subunits
questions.explain: The 19S regulatory cap recognises polyubiquitin chains (via its ubiquitin receptor), removes the ubiquitin tags (via deubiquitinase), and uses ATP to unfold the substrate. Proteolytic cleavage is performed exclusively by the 20S core particle. Acidification and glycosylation are lysosomal, not proteasomal, features.

questions.q: In which cellular locations is the 26S proteasome found?
options: Only within lysosomes
Only in the endoplasmic reticulum lumen
Only in the mitochondrial matrix
Throughout the cytoplasm and nucleoplasm, concentrated at the nuclear envelope–ER network
Exclusively on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane
questions.explain: The proteasome is distributed throughout the cytoplasm and nucleoplasm and is particularly concentrated at the nuclear envelope–ER network. This distribution allows it to degrade misfolded proteins as they emerge from ribosomes in both compartments.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of events during proteasomal degradation of a ubiquitinated protein?
