options: Ubiquitin removal → polyubiquitin recognition → ATP-driven unfolding → threading into 20S → peptide release
Polyubiquitin recognition → ubiquitin removal → ATP-driven unfolding → threading into 20S → peptide release
ATP-driven unfolding → polyubiquitin recognition → ubiquitin removal → threading into 20S → peptide release
Threading into 20S → ubiquitin removal → peptide release → ATP-driven unfolding
Polyubiquitin recognition → threading into 20S → ATP-driven unfolding → ubiquitin removal → peptide release
questions.explain: The correct order is: (1) 19S ubiquitin receptor recognises the polyubiquitin chain, (2) deubiquitinase removes and recycles Ub tags, (3) 19S ATPases unfold the protein, (4) unfolded protein is threaded into the 20S core, (5) proteases generate peptide fragments. Ubiquitin removal precedes unfolding so the Ub tags are recycled before degradation.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of ubiquitin (Ub) tagging in cells?
options: Targeting Lys48-linked polyubiquitin substrates to the proteasome
Routing Lys63-linked ubiquitinated receptors through the endocytic pathway to lysosomes
Directing ESCRT complexes to sort cargo into intraluminal vesicles of MVBs
Serving as the direct proteolytic enzyme that degrades the target protein
Signalling protein quality control through polyubiquitin chain formation
questions.explain: Ubiquitin is a tag — it marks proteins for degradation but does not itself perform proteolytic cleavage. Actual degradation is carried out by the proteasome or lysosomal hydrolases. All other options correctly describe ubiquitin's roles in routing proteins to different degradation compartments.

questions.q: What is the approximate diameter and luminal pH of a typical lysosome?
options: 2–5 μm diameter; pH 7.2
0.2–0.5 μm diameter; pH 5.0
0.2–0.5 μm diameter; pH 7.2
1–2 μm diameter; pH 5.0
10 nm diameter; pH 4.0
questions.explain: Lysosomes are 0.2–0.5 μm in diameter with a luminal pH of approximately 5.0 (acidic). The cytosol maintains pH ~7.2. This acidic environment is essential for the activity of the >50 types of acid hydrolases inside the lysosome.

questions.q: Why are the H⁺ pump and transport proteins of the lysosomal membrane heavily glycosylated on their luminal face?
options: To increase the rate of H⁺ transport into the lumen
To facilitate fusion of the lysosome with autophagosomes
To protect them from degradation by the luminal acid hydrolases
To allow binding of ubiquitinated cargo at the membrane surface
To lower the pH of the lysosome lumen more rapidly
questions.explain: The luminal face of lysosomal membrane proteins is heavily glycosylated as a protective sugar coat that shields the proteins from the very acid hydrolases they help to contain. Without this protection, the membrane proteins would themselves be degraded. Glycosylation does not directly drive H⁺ transport or membrane fusion.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly pairs a lysosomal delivery pathway with its cargo?
options: Phagocytosis — extracellular proteins taken up by coated vesicles
Endocytosis — bacteria and large particles engulfed by phagosomes
Secretory pathway — lysosomal enzymes from the trans-Golgi network
Autophagy — receptor tyrosine kinases recycled back to the plasma membrane
Phagocytosis — lysosomal hydrolases synthesised in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: The secretory pathway delivers newly synthesised lysosomal enzymes from the ER through the Golgi, via the trans-Golgi network (TGN), to late endosomes and then lysosomes. Phagocytosis handles bacteria and large particles; endocytosis handles extracellular proteins and receptors; autophagy handles organelles and protein aggregates.

questions.q: A mutant cell line lacks ESCRT complexes. Which of the following lysosomal delivery pathways would be MOST directly disrupted?
options: Phagocytosis of bacteria
Secretory pathway delivery of lysosomal enzymes from TGN
Macroautophagy of damaged mitochondria
Endocytic routing of ubiquitinated receptors into multivesicular bodies
Chaperone-mediated autophagy via LAMP-2A
questions.explain: ESCRT complexes (ESCRT-I, -II, -III) recognise ubiquitin tags on membrane receptors and sort them into intraluminal vesicles of the MVB (multivesicular body). Loss of ESCRT function specifically disrupts this endocytic receptor degradation pathway. Phagocytosis, autophagy, and CMA do not require ESCRT complexes.

questions.q: Yoshinori Ohsumi was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2016 for discovering which cellular process?
options: Ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation
Lysosomal biogenesis and acid hydrolase trafficking
Autophagy mechanisms
ESCRT-mediated MVB sorting
Chaperone-mediated protein folding
questions.explain: Yoshinori Ohsumi received the 2016 Nobel Prize for his discoveries of the mechanisms of autophagy — the cellular "self-eating" process. The ubiquitin-proteasome Nobel was awarded to Ciechanover, Hershko, and Rose in 2004.

questions.q: Which type of autophagy involves direct invagination of the lysosomal membrane to engulf small cytoplasmic portions, without forming an autophagosome?
options: Macroautophagy
Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA)
Microautophagy
Mitophagy
Xenophagy
questions.explain: Microautophagy involves direct invagination of the lysosomal membrane to engulf small cytoplasmic material. CMA uses Hsc70 to translocate individual proteins across the membrane via LAMP-2A, while macroautophagy forms a double-membrane autophagosome around larger cargo.

questions.q: In chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), which two components are essential for recognising and translocating the substrate protein into the lysosome?
options: Atg8/LC3 and the HOPS tethering complex
Hsc70 chaperone and LAMP-2A channel
Rab7 GTPase and SNARE proteins
E3 ubiquitin ligase and 19S regulatory cap
ESCRT complexes and Rab5 GTPase
questions.explain: In CMA, the cytosolic chaperone Hsc70 recognises a KFERQ-like motif on the substrate protein and delivers it to LAMP-2A, a channel on the lysosomal membrane, for direct translocation into the lumen. No autophagosome is formed. Atg8/LC3 and HOPS are macroautophagy components.

questions.q: What is the FIRST step of macroautophagy?
options: Fusion of the autophagosome with the lysosome
Vesicle nucleation — formation of the isolation membrane (phagophore) requiring PI3P and ATG proteins
Coating of the phagophore membrane with ATG8/LC3
Activation of the Mon1-Ccz1 GEF complex on Rab7
Breakdown of cargo by lysosomal hydrolases
questions.explain: Macroautophagy begins with vesicle nucleation — the formation of the isolation membrane (phagophore) requires PI3P (phosphatidylinositol-3-phosphate) and ATG proteins. Subsequent steps include elongation (LC3 coating), docking/fusion with the lysosome (HOPS/SNARE), and finally degradation.

questions.q: During macroautophagy, which molecular machinery is required for the docking and fusion of the autophagosome with the lysosome/vacuole?
options: ESCRT-I, -II, -III complexes and Rab5
E1/E2/E3 ubiquitin cascade and the 19S cap
Rab7/Ypt7 GTPase, HOPS tethering complex, and SNARE proteins
LAMP-2A channel and Hsc70 chaperone
20S proteasome core and deubiquitinase
questions.explain: Autophagosome–lysosome fusion requires: Ypt7/Rab7 GTPase (activated by Mon1-Ccz1 GEF), the HOPS tethering complex, and SNARE proteins (e.g., Ykt6). ESCRT complexes are used in MVB sorting, not autophagy fusion. LAMP-2A is specific to CMA.

questions.q: The Mon1-Ccz1 complex acts as a GEF (guanine nucleotide exchange factor). What is its direct substrate and what does it activate?
options: Atg8/LC3; it promotes phagophore elongation
SNARE protein Ykt6; it initiates membrane fusion
Rab GTPase Ypt7 (Rab7 in mammals); it switches Ypt7 from GDP-bound to GTP-bound active form
HOPS complex subunit Vps39; it recruits the tethering complex to the autophagosome
Beclin1; it initiates vesicle nucleation at the ER
questions.explain: Mon1-Ccz1 is a GEF that activates the Rab GTPase Ypt7 (Rab7 in mammals) by exchanging GDP for GTP. Active Ypt7-GTP then binds the HOPS tethering complex on the vacuole membrane, initiating tethering and subsequent SNARE-mediated fusion. Mon1-Ccz1 is recruited to the autophagosome via Atg8/LC3.

questions.q: A researcher generates a yeast mutant that lacks a functional HOPS tethering complex. Which phenotype would you expect?
options: Autophagosomes fail to form the double-membrane structure
LC3/Atg8 fails to coat the phagophore membrane
Autophagosomes accumulate outside the vacuole because fusion cannot occur
Ubiquitinated cargo is redirected to the proteasome
The lysosomal H⁺ pump is inactivated and pH rises to 7.2
questions.explain: HOPS mutants specifically fail at the docking/fusion step. Autophagosomes complete formation but cannot fuse with the vacuole, so they accumulate as Atg8 dots outside the vacuole lumen — a phenotype confirmed experimentally. Loss of Atg8 coating or phagophore formation would result from defects in nucleation/elongation steps.

questions.q: How does autophagy play a DUAL role in cancer — and what determines which role it plays?
options: Autophagy always suppresses cancer by removing all misfolded proteins before they mutate DNA.
Autophagy always promotes cancer by helping tumour cells survive chemotherapy.
Autophagy is tumour-suppressive in early tumourigenesis (prevents malignant transformation) but pro-survival in established tumours (helps cells tolerate stress).
Autophagy is irrelevant to cancer; its role is limited to starvation responses.
Autophagy suppresses cancer only when Beclin1 is absent; it promotes cancer only when Beclin1 is overexpressed.
questions.explain: Autophagy has a context-dependent role: in early tumourigenesis it acts as a tumour suppressor by eliminating damaged organelles and proteins that could drive malignant transformation. In established tumours, cancer cells exploit autophagy as a survival mechanism to endure metabolic stress and resist chemotherapy. Option E reverses the Beclin1 relationship.

questions.q: In the Liang et al. (1999) Nature study using MCF7 breast carcinoma cells, what was the tumour incidence in mice injected with Beclin1-deficient MCF7 cells by day 5?
options: 0% — tumours did not form without Beclin1
~50% — half of mice developed tumours
~67% — 6 out of 9 mice developed tumours
~89% — 8 out of 9 mice developed tumours
100% — all mice developed tumours
questions.explain: In Liang et al. (1999), ~89% (8/9) of mice injected with Beclin1-deficient MCF7 cells developed tumours by day 5, demonstrating that loss of autophagy (via Beclin1 deletion) strongly promotes tumour growth. MCF7 cells with Beclin1 restored showed significantly reduced tumour incidence.

lectures.title: Lecture 15: The Cytoskeleton and Cell Movement I

questions.q: Which of the following lists the three principal cytoskeletal filaments in order of increasing diameter?
options: Actin filaments (~7 nm) → intermediate filaments (~10 nm) → microtubules (~25 nm)
Intermediate filaments (~10 nm) → actin filaments (~7 nm) → microtubules (~25 nm)
Actin filaments (~7 nm) → microtubules (~25 nm) → intermediate filaments (~10 nm)
Microtubules (~25 nm) → intermediate filaments (~10 nm) → actin filaments (~7 nm)
Intermediate filaments (~10 nm) → microtubules (~25 nm) → actin filaments (~7 nm)
questions.explain: The correct ascending order is actin (~7 nm), intermediate filaments (~10 nm), and microtubules (~25 nm). A common mistake is assuming intermediate filaments are the largest because of their name, but microtubules are the widest at ~25 nm.

questions.q: Which statement about the cytoskeleton is CORRECT?
options: The cytoskeleton is found in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes
Only microtubules are dynamic; actin and intermediate filaments are permanently stable
Actin filaments are the largest of the three principal filament types
All three principal cytoskeletal filament types can polymerize and depolymerize in response to cell signals
The cytoskeleton is confined to the nucleus and does not extend into the cytoplasm
questions.explain: All three cytoskeletal filament types are dynamic — they polymerize and depolymerize in response to cell signals. Bacteria lack true intermediate filaments and microtubules; the cytoskeleton is eukaryote-specific and extends throughout the cytoplasm, not confined to the nucleus.

questions.q: What is the primary subunit of actin filaments?
options: α/β-tubulin heterodimer
Lamin monomer
γ-tubulin monomer
Keratin dimer
G-actin monomer
questions.explain: Actin filaments are built from G-actin (globular actin) monomers. α/β-tubulin heterodimers are the subunits of microtubules, while lamins and keratins are intermediate filament subunits.

questions.q: Which feature distinguishes intermediate filaments from both actin filaments and microtubules?
options: Intermediate filaments require GTP hydrolysis for assembly
Intermediate filaments are the thickest of the three filament types
Motor proteins preferentially walk along intermediate filaments for cargo transport
Intermediate filaments are polar structures with distinct plus and minus ends
Intermediate filaments are non-polar and do not require nucleotide hydrolysis for assembly
questions.explain: Intermediate filaments are non-polar (no plus/minus end) and assemble without ATP or GTP hydrolysis. Actin and microtubules are polar and require nucleotide hydrolysis. Because IFs lack polarity, motor proteins cannot use them as directional tracks.

questions.q: Epidermolysis bullosa simplex is caused by mutations in which gene, and what is the resulting cellular defect?
options: Lamin gene mutations → nuclear lamina breakdown → cell death
Keratin gene mutations → defective IF network in basal epidermal cells → blistered skin upon mechanical stress
α-tubulin gene mutations → disrupted mitotic spindle → abnormal cell division
Actin gene mutations → loss of cell cortex → rupture of internal membranes
Dynein gene mutations → impaired retrograde transport → accumulation of vesicles at cell periphery
questions.explain: Mutations in keratin genes disrupt the intermediate filament network in basal cells of the epidermis. Without a robust IF network, these cells cannot withstand mechanical friction, causing the skin to blister. Lamin mutations affect nuclear integrity but do not cause this particular blistering disease.

questions.q: At which cellular junctions are intermediate filaments anchored to link the cytoskeletons of adjacent cells?
options: Tight junctions
Gap junctions
Focal adhesions
Adherens junctions
Desmosomes
questions.explain: Intermediate filaments are anchored at desmosomes (cell-cell junctions), which physically links the IF networks of neighboring cells to distribute tensile forces across a tissue. IFs are also anchored at hemidesmosomes to the basal lamina, not at tight junctions or gap junctions.

questions.q: The nuclear lamina is composed of which type of protein, and where is it located?
options: Actin; lining the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane
Lamins (a type of intermediate filament protein); on the nucleoplasmic face of the inner nuclear membrane
β-tubulin; forming a cage around chromosomes in the nucleus
Myosin II; providing contractile force to the nucleus
Keratin; anchoring chromatin to the outer nuclear membrane
questions.explain: The nuclear lamina is a 10–40 nm thick meshwork of lamins, which are a specialized type of intermediate filament protein, located on the nucleoplasmic surface of the inner nuclear membrane. It provides structural integrity to the nucleus.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the structure of a microtubule?
options: A solid rod of 7 protofilaments made of actin monomers, ~7 nm in diameter
A twisted rope of coiled-coil dimers, ~10 nm in diameter
A hollow cylinder of 13 protofilaments made of α/β-tubulin heterodimers, ~25 nm in diameter
A hollow cylinder of 9 protofilaments made of γ-tubulin subunits, ~25 nm in diameter
A solid rod of 13 protofilaments made of lamin monomers, ~10 nm in diameter
questions.explain: Microtubules are hollow cylindrical tubes ~25 nm in diameter whose walls consist of 13 protofilaments, each made of repeating α/β-tubulin heterodimers. Actin filaments and intermediate filaments have distinct diameters (~7 nm and ~10 nm, respectively) and different subunit compositions.

questions.q: At the plus end of a growing microtubule, which tubulin subunit is exposed, and which nucleotide does it carry?
options: α-tubulin exposed; carries exchangeable GTP
γ-tubulin exposed; carries GDP
α-tubulin exposed; carries GDP
β-tubulin exposed; carries exchangeable GTP
β-tubulin exposed; carries non-exchangeable GTP
questions.explain: The plus end exposes β-tubulin, which carries exchangeable GTP that forms the stabilizing GTP cap. The GTP bound to α-tubulin is non-exchangeable and plays no role in dynamic instability. Confusing α- and β-tubulin's nucleotide states is a common exam error.

questions.q: Which of the following accurately describes the polarity of the three cytoskeletal filament types?
options: Actin filaments are non-polar; microtubules and intermediate filaments are polar
All three filament types are polar with distinct plus and minus ends
Intermediate filaments are polar; actin filaments and microtubules are non-polar
All three filament types are non-polar
Actin filaments and microtubules are polar; intermediate filaments are non-polar
questions.explain: Both actin filaments and microtubules are polar structures with biochemically distinct plus and minus ends, which is essential for directional motor protein movement. Intermediate filaments, by contrast, are non-polar — they have no plus or minus end.

questions.q: During dynamic instability, what event is called 'catastrophe'?
options: The re-formation of a GTP cap at the plus end of a shrinking microtubule
The complete severing of a microtubule by katanin enzyme
The attachment of a motor protein to the microtubule minus end
The transition of a microtubule from the minus end to the plus end during polymerization
The loss of the GTP cap due to hydrolysis outpacing tubulin addition, leading to rapid depolymerization
questions.explain: Catastrophe occurs when GTP hydrolysis at the plus end proceeds faster than new GTP-tubulin subunits are added, causing the GTP cap to be lost. The exposed GDP-tubulin lattice is curved and unstable, triggering rapid depolymerization. Rescue (re-formation of a GTP cap) is the opposite event.

questions.q: If a cell is treated so that only GDP (no GTP) is available, what will happen to its microtubules?
options: Microtubules will polymerize continuously because GDP-tubulin is more stable than GTP-tubulin
Microtubules will be unaffected because GDP is not involved in microtubule dynamics
Microtubules will shrink completely because no GTP cap can form to stabilize the plus end
Microtubules will become permanently stabilized as GDP locks the protofilaments in a straight conformation
Microtubules will convert to actin filaments when GTP is unavailable
questions.explain: Without GTP, incoming tubulin dimers cannot carry the GTP needed to form or maintain a stabilizing cap at the plus end. GDP-tubulin in the lattice adopts a curved conformation, making the polymer unstable. As a result, all microtubules undergo catastrophe and shrink.

questions.q: What is the functional significance of dynamic instability in living cells?
options: It permanently stabilizes microtubules so they can form a rigid scaffold
It allows microtubules to rapidly explore cellular space and be selectively stabilized at specific locations
It provides the mechanical resistance needed to withstand tensile stress across tissues
It generates the power stroke required for actin-based cell crawling
It enables intermediate filaments to switch between polar and non-polar configurations
questions.explain: Dynamic instability allows a single microtubule to oscillate stochastically between growth and shrinkage, rapidly exploring cellular space. Cells exploit this by selectively stabilizing microtubules at specific locations (e.g., at kinetochores during mitosis), building arrays with precisely defined shapes.

questions.q: Which experimental condition would produce microtubules that grow continuously WITHOUT undergoing catastrophe?
options: Addition of excess GDP to the polymerization buffer
Treatment with a drug that blocks GTP binding to α-tubulin
Removal of all γ-tubulin from the centrosome matrix
Use of a non-hydrolyzable GTP analog so tubulin is always capped
Addition of katanin to sever microtubules at intervals
questions.explain: A non-hydrolyzable GTP analog cannot be converted to GDP, so the plus end always retains a stable GTP cap. Without hydrolysis, the trigger for catastrophe never occurs and microtubules grow continuously. Excess GDP has the opposite effect, causing continuous shrinkage.

questions.q: What is the role of γ-tubulin ring complexes (γ-TuRC) at the centrosome?
options: To form the lattice of the elongating microtubule wall alongside α/β-tubulin
To provide the GTP cap at the plus end of growing microtubules
To serve as the template for nucleating new microtubules by presenting 13 γ-tubulin subunits matching the 13-protofilament arrangement
To cross-link adjacent microtubules in the axoneme of cilia
To power minus-end-directed transport of organelles toward the nucleus
questions.explain: γ-TuRC presents 13 γ-tubulin subunits arranged in a circle that mirrors the 13-protofilament geometry of a microtubule. This template nucleates polymerization, bypassing the slow spontaneous nucleation step. γ-tubulin itself does NOT become incorporated into the growing microtubule lattice.

questions.q: How many γ-TuSC units assemble to form one γ-TuRC, and how many γ-tubulin subunits does this expose?
options: 9 γ-TuSC units → 18 γ-tubulin subunits
13 γ-TuSC units → 26 γ-tubulin subunits
2 γ-TuSC units → 4 γ-tubulin subunits
7 γ-TuSC units → 14 γ-tubulin subunits
7 γ-TuSC units → 13 γ-tubulin subunits
questions.explain: Seven copies of γ-TuSC (each containing 2 γ-tubulin subunits plus accessory proteins) assemble into a spiral, exposing exactly 13 γ-tubulin subunits — matching the 13 protofilaments of a microtubule. This arrangement provides the nucleation template.

questions.q: In an interphase animal cell, where is the minus end of most microtubules located, and why?
options: At the cell periphery (cortex), because plus ends radiate inward from there
Embedded in the centrosome near the nucleus, because the centrosome is the MTOC that anchors minus ends
At the kinetochore of each chromosome, because chromosomes capture minus ends during mitosis
At the basal body of each cilium, because basal bodies nucleate minus ends for cytoplasmic transport
Free in the cytoplasm, because minus ends are not anchored in interphase cells
questions.explain: The centrosome, as the main MTOC in animal cells, anchors the minus ends of microtubules. Plus ends radiate outward toward the cell periphery. This orientation establishes directional cues for kinesin (plus-end directed, toward periphery) and dynein (minus-end directed, toward centrosome).

questions.q: Which structural components make up the centrosome?
options: A pair of centrioles surrounded by a pericentriolar matrix containing γ-TuRC
A single centriole surrounded by actin filaments and myosin II
Nine doublet microtubules arranged in a ring, identical to a ciliary axoneme
A ring of γ-tubulin monomers with no surrounding protein matrix
Two basal bodies connected by a transition zone and embedded in the plasma membrane
questions.explain: The centrosome consists of a pair of centrioles (short cylinders built from triplet microtubules) embedded in a protein-rich pericentriolar matrix. The matrix contains γ-TuRC complexes that nucleate new microtubules. The 9+2 arrangement describes cilia/flagella axonemes, not centrosomes.

questions.q: Kinesin and cytoplasmic dynein both walk along microtubules using ATP hydrolysis. How do they differ in directionality?
options: Kinesin moves toward the minus end; dynein moves toward the plus end
Kinesin moves toward the plus end (periphery); dynein moves toward the minus end (centrosome)
Both move toward the plus end but at different speeds
Kinesin moves laterally between protofilaments; dynein moves longitudinally
Both move toward the minus end, but kinesin dissociates before reaching the centrosome
questions.explain: Conventional kinesin-1 is a plus-end-directed motor, moving cargo from the centrosome toward the cell periphery (anterograde). Cytoplasmic dynein is a minus-end-directed motor, moving cargo back toward the centrosome (retrograde). Reversing these is one of the most common exam errors.

questions.q: In fish skin cells, melanosomes (pigment granules) can either disperse to the periphery or aggregate at the center. Which motors are responsible for each movement?
options: Dynein → dispersal to periphery; kinesin → aggregation at center
Both dynein and kinesin move melanosomes to the periphery; actin mediates central aggregation
Myosin moves melanosomes to the periphery; kinesin mediates central aggregation
Melanosomes are moved by actin filaments in both directions, not by microtubule motors
Kinesin → dispersal to periphery (plus-end directed); dynein → aggregation at center (minus-end directed)
questions.explain: Dispersal of melanosomes outward (toward the periphery) is driven by kinesin, a plus-end-directed motor. Aggregation toward the center (minus end / centrosome) is driven by dynein, a minus-end-directed motor. This model is a classic example of bidirectional organelle transport on microtubule tracks.

questions.q: Which domain of a motor protein binds cargo being transported, and which domain contacts the microtubule?
options: Tail domain contacts microtubule; globular head domain binds cargo
Stalk domain contacts microtubule; globular head domain binds cargo
Both domains contact the microtubule; cargo is bound by accessory proteins only
Globular head domain contacts microtubule (ATPase domain); tail domain binds cargo
Globular head domain binds cargo; coiled-coil stalk contacts microtubule
questions.explain: Motor proteins have a globular head domain (the ATPase motor domain) that contacts and walks along the microtubule, and a tail domain that binds to specific cargo (vesicles, organelles, chromosomes, etc.). Reversing these domains is a common confusion.

questions.q: Which of the following types of cargo is NOT transported along microtubules by motor proteins in the scenarios described in the lecture?
options: Secretory vesicles moving from the Golgi to the plasma membrane
Mitochondria repositioned in response to metabolic demand
Collagen monomers crossing the plasma membrane by exocytosis
Melanosomes distributing pigment within skin cells
Chromosomes being pulled toward spindle poles during mitosis
questions.explain: Collagen monomer secretion by exocytosis involves vesicle fusion at the plasma membrane, but the exocytosis step itself (the actual crossing of the membrane) is mediated by SNARE proteins, not by motor protein-driven microtubule transport. All other options are classic examples of microtubule-based motor transport.

questions.q: What is the '9+2' arrangement in the axoneme of a motile cilium?
options: 9 actin protofilaments surrounding 2 intermediate filament bundles
9 singlet microtubules surrounding 2 central doublet microtubules
9 triplet microtubules surrounding 2 γ-TuRC complexes
9 dynein arms arranged around 2 radial spoke arrays
9 outer doublet microtubules surrounding 2 central singlet microtubules
questions.explain: The axoneme of a motile cilium has a 9+2 arrangement: 9 outer doublet microtubules arranged in a ring, each bearing dynein arms, surrounding 2 central singlet microtubules. The basal body has 9 triplet (not doublet) microtubules and no central pair.

questions.q: How does axonemal dynein generate the bending motion of a cilium in an intact cell, as opposed to isolated doublets?
options: In isolated doublets with ATP, dynein slides adjacent doublets; in intact cilia, linking proteins resist sliding and convert the force into bending
In intact cilia, dynein directly bends the doublet microtubules by changing its own shape; in isolated doublets it cannot function
In intact cilia, dynein rotates the central pair microtubules; isolated doublets cannot rotate
In both isolated and intact conditions, dynein generates bending; only the speed differs
In isolated doublets, dynein causes bending; intact cilia use kinesin to generate the power stroke
questions.explain: When doublet microtubules are isolated with ATP, axonemal dynein slides adjacent doublets relative to each other (translational sliding). In an intact cilium, protein links between doublets resist sliding, so the force generated by dynein is redirected into bending of the entire axoneme. Without linking proteins, bending cannot occur.

questions.q: What is the structural relationship between the basal body and a centriole?
options: Basal bodies have a 9+2 doublet arrangement; centrioles have a 9+0 arrangement
Centrioles contain a central pair of microtubules; basal bodies do not
Basal bodies and centrioles are structurally identical, both consisting of 9 sets of triplet microtubules
Basal bodies are made of actin filaments; centrioles are made of microtubules
Centrioles have 13 protofilaments per triplet; basal bodies have 9 protofilaments per triplet
questions.explain: The basal body, which sits at the base of every cilium and acts as the MTOC for axoneme assembly, is structurally identical to a centriole — both consist of 9 sets of triplet microtubules. The 9+2 arrangement (doublets) is found in the axoneme above the basal body, not in the basal body itself.

questions.q: Primary (immotile) cilia differ functionally from motile cilia primarily because they:
options: Have a 9+2 axoneme arrangement and produce coordinated beating to move mucus
Are found only on sperm cells and generate rotational propulsion
Are powered by kinesin rather than dynein and transport vesicles along the axoneme
Lack dynein arms and function as sensory antennae, transducing environmental signals
Contain actin filaments instead of microtubules and mediate cell-substrate adhesion
questions.explain: Primary cilia are immotile because they typically lack functional dynein arms. Instead, they serve as sensory antennae — their membranes and axonemes contain receptors that transduce signals such as fluid flow (renal cilia) or odorants (olfactory cilia). Motile cilia beat back and forth to move overlying fluid such as mucus.

questions.q: A patient presents with chronic recurrent respiratory infections, male infertility, and chronic sinusitis. Which cellular defect is most likely responsible?
options: Keratin gene mutation causing fragile epithelial cells
Loss of nuclear lamin causing nuclear envelope instability
Excess γ-TuRC activity causing abnormal microtubule nucleation
Kinesin-1 mutation blocking anterograde vesicle transport in neurons
Defective axonemal dynein in cilia lining the respiratory tract and reproductive tract
questions.explain: This clinical picture is classic for primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD), caused by defects in cilia (often axonemal dynein mutations). Impaired mucociliary clearance leads to chronic respiratory infections, and dysfunctional reproductive cilia/sperm flagella cause infertility. Keratin mutations cause blistered skin, not this constellation of symptoms.

questions.q: Which filament type is MOST suited to resist mechanical stretching forces in epithelial tissues, and what structural feature enables this?
options: Microtubules; their 13-protofilament hollow tube provides compressive rigidity
Actin filaments; their polar structure enables force transmission in one direction
Intermediate filaments; their non-polar rope structure and anchorage at desmosomes enables tensile force distribution across cells
Microtubules; their dynamic instability allows them to absorb mechanical energy by depolymerizing
Actin filaments; their cross-linking by filamin creates a gel-like cortex that resists deformation
questions.explain: Intermediate filaments form rope-shaped non-polar structures that excel at resisting tensile (stretching) forces. Anchored to desmosomes between adjacent cells and to hemidesmosomes at the basal lamina, they distribute mechanical stress across the entire tissue. Microtubules are rigid but more suited to resisting compression and serving as transport tracks.

questions.q: In the sequence of events during microtubule nucleation at the centrosome, which occurs FIRST?
options: α/β-tubulin heterodimers spontaneously polymerize to form a short seed filament in the cytoplasm
Kinesin motor proteins deliver tubulin dimers to the centrosome from the cell periphery
The minus end of an existing microtubule detaches from the centrosome and migrates to the plus end
γ-TuSC subunits assemble into γ-TuRC within the pericentriolar matrix, creating the nucleation template
GDP-tubulin accumulates at the centrosome and is converted to GTP-tubulin by local kinases
questions.explain: Microtubule nucleation at the centrosome begins when γ-TuSC units assemble into a γ-TuRC ring template within the pericentriolar matrix. This template then recruits free α/β-tubulin heterodimers from the cytoplasm, bypassing the thermodynamically unfavorable spontaneous nucleation step. GDP-tubulin conversion by kinases is not part of this mechanism.

questions.q: Which of the following comparisons between kinesin and intermediate filaments is CORRECT?
options: Both kinesin and intermediate filaments require GTP hydrolysis for their function
Both kinesin and intermediate filaments are found in prokaryotes as well as eukaryotes
Intermediate filaments, like kinesin, use ATP hydrolysis to generate mechanical force
Kinesin moves along intermediate filaments toward the minus end of the filament
Kinesin is a polar motor protein that requires track polarity; intermediate filaments are non-polar and cannot serve as kinesin tracks
questions.explain: Kinesin is a directional motor that requires polar microtubule tracks (distinct plus and minus ends) to move specifically toward the plus end. Intermediate filaments are non-polar — they lack plus and minus ends — making it structurally impossible for motor proteins such as kinesin to walk along them directionally.

lectures.title: Lecture 16: The Cytoskeleton and Cell Movement II

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the polarity of F-actin filaments?
options: Both ends are chemically identical and grow at the same rate
The plus end (barbed end) grows faster than the minus end (pointed end) due to asymmetric monomer orientation
The minus end is exposed to the plus end of adjacent filaments in the cytoskeleton
Polarity is determined by the GTP/GDP state of the terminal monomer
F-actin has no intrinsic polarity; directionality is imposed only by binding proteins
questions.explain: G-actin monomers are asymmetric and polymerize head-to-tail, creating structural polarity. The plus (barbed) end grows faster than the minus (pointed) end. This polarity is essential for directional transport and cell movement.

questions.q: In the context of actin filaments, what is the role of ATP hydrolysis?
options: It provides the energy for actin polymerization at the plus end
It weakens inter-subunit bonds in ADP-actin, promoting depolymerization and enabling treadmilling
It prevents spontaneous nucleation by capping both filament ends
It activates profilin to accelerate monomer addition
It directly stabilizes the F-actin helix structure
questions.explain: ATP hydrolysis does not provide energy for polymerization; instead, it weakens bonds to promote disassembly and supports treadmilling. This parallels GTP hydrolysis in tubulin. A common exam trap is confusing ATP hydrolysis as the driving force for assembly.

questions.q: Which condition defines actin treadmilling?
options: Both plus and minus ends simultaneously elongate while ATP hydrolysis occurs slowly
ATP-actin adds to the plus end at a rate faster than ATP hydrolysis, while ADP-actin falls off the minus end
Gelsolin severs filaments and both fragments depolymerize from their new plus ends
The minus end grows faster than the plus end, causing the filament to reverse orientation
All actin subunits hydrolyze ATP simultaneously, leading to rapid disassembly
questions.explain: Treadmilling occurs when plus-end addition rate > ATP hydrolysis rate > minus-end addition rate. Subunits move through the filament from plus to minus end while net length remains constant. This is critical for cell migration.

questions.q: What is the primary function of thymosin in actin regulation?
options: It nucleates actin filament assembly at the leading edge of migrating cells
It sequesters G-actin monomers to maintain a free monomer pool and prevent uncontrolled assembly
It caps the plus end of F-actin to prevent further polymerization
It promotes ATP hydrolysis to accelerate treadmilling turnover
It severs existing actin filaments in response to calcium signals
questions.explain: Thymosin binds and sequesters G-actin, controlling the available monomer pool. This prevents spontaneous polymerization and allows cells to rapidly mobilize actin when needed.

questions.q: How do formin and Arp2/3 differ in their mechanisms of nucleation?
options: Formin caps the minus end; Arp2/3 caps the plus end
Formin nucleates linear unbranched filaments and remains at the plus end; Arp2/3 nucleates branched filaments and stays at the minus end of each new branch
Formin requires calcium activation; Arp2/3 is calcium-independent
Formin sequesters monomers; Arp2/3 severs existing filaments
Formin generates branches at 70° angles; Arp2/3 generates perpendicular branches
questions.explain: This distinction is frequently tested and confused. Formin produces linear filaments (like stress fibers and filopodia) and stays processively at the plus end. Arp2/3 creates branched dendritic networks (like in lamellipodia) and anchors at the minus end of newly nucleated branches.

questions.q: Which actin-binding protein accelerates depolymerization and promotes treadmilling turnover?
options: Profilin
Capping protein
Cofilin
Tropomodulin
Gelsolin
questions.explain: Cofilin binds ADP-actin in older filament regions and accelerates disassembly, promoting treadmilling. This allows rapid monomer turnover essential for cell migration.

questions.q: What is the structural difference between a lamellipodium and a filopodium?
options: Lamellipodia contain parallel actin bundles; filopodia contain branched actin networks
Lamellipodia are found only in neuronal growth cones; filopodia are found in all migrating cells
Lamellipodia contain Arp2/3-nucleated branched actin meshwork; filopodia contain formin-nucleated parallel actin bundles
Filopodia are much thicker and shorter than lamellipodia
Lamellipodia form during the contraction phase; filopodia form during protrusion
questions.explain: Lamellipodia are flat, broad protrusions with a branched actin network (Arp2/3 nucleated). Filopodia are thin, finger-like projections containing parallel actin bundles (formin nucleated). Both extend from the leading edge but serve different functions.

questions.q: Which structure serves as the primary link between the actin cytoskeleton and the extracellular matrix during cell migration?
options: Tropomyosin
Focal adhesions containing integrin proteins
The sarcoplasmic reticulum
T tubules
Centrioles
questions.explain: Focal adhesions (focal contacts) are integrin-containing anchors that link the actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix. They transmit traction forces and are essential for directed cell movement.

questions.q: In the four-step cell crawling cycle, which phase involves myosin-II-mediated contraction of the actin cortex?
options: Protrusion
Attachment
Contraction, where myosin-II slides actin filaments and the cell body moves forward
Retraction
Nucleation
questions.explain: The contraction phase uses myosin-II in the actin cortex to generate force that pulls the cell body forward. Simultaneously, unpolymerized actin flows toward the leading edge for reuse in the next protrusion cycle.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of events in the cell crawling cycle?
options: Attachment → Protrusion → Contraction → Retraction
Protrusion → Attachment → Contraction → Retraction
Retraction → Contraction → Attachment → Protrusion
Protrusion → Contraction → Attachment → Retraction
Attachment → Retraction → Protrusion → Contraction
questions.explain: The canonical order is protrusion (actin polymerization at leading edge), attachment (via integrins), contraction (myosin-II in actin cortex), and retraction (rear focal adhesion release). This cycle repeats for directional migration.

questions.q: How does Listeria monocytogenes achieve intracellular motility without using myosin?
options: It activates intermediate filaments to generate pulling forces
It uses kinesin and dynein motors on microtubules for transport
Its ActA protein activates Arp2/3 to nucleate a dendritic actin comet tail; polymerization alone generates propulsive force
It severs actin filaments via gelsolin to create momentum
It hijacks the host cell's myosin-I motors
questions.explain: Listeria's ActA protein nucleates Arp2/3-driven branched actin assembly at one bacterial pole, creating a comet tail. Cofilin depolymerizes the older (ADP) actin regions at the tail rear. Polymerization force alone, without myosin, propels the bacterium forward.

questions.q: Which statement about bacterial actin-based motility is correct?
options: Myosin-II is required to generate the propulsive force
The bacterial surface protein ActA mimics host formin function
Actin polymerization, without myosin, is sufficient to move a bacterial cell through the cytoplasm
Capping protein stabilizes the comet tail at the bacterial plus end
The process requires simultaneous activation of both Arp2/3 and formin
questions.explain: A key insight from Listeria motility is that actin polymerization alone can generate sufficient force to move objects. This contrasts with most cell migration, which relies on myosin. This system demonstrates the intrinsic power of the cytoskeleton.

questions.q: What is the primary structural difference between myosin-I and myosin-II?
options: Myosin-I has a long tail; myosin-II has a short tail
Myosin-I is single-headed (~70 nm) and transports cargo; myosin-II is double-headed (~150 nm) and forms bipolar filaments for contraction
Myosin-I is found only in muscle; myosin-II is found in all cell types
Myosin-I moves toward the minus end of actin; myosin-II moves toward the plus end
Myosin-I requires troponin for regulation; myosin-II does not
questions.explain: Myosin-I (~70 nm) has one head domain and a tail that attaches to membranes or vesicles, transporting cargo along actin toward the plus end. Myosin-II (~150 nm) has two heads and a coiled-coil tail, assembling into bipolar filaments essential for muscle contraction and cytokinesis.

questions.q: How is myosin-II organized in bipolar filaments, and what is the function of the bare region?
options: Heads overlap at the center; the bare region is where tails interact to strengthen the filament
Molecules associate head-to-head with tails pointing outward; the bare region contains tails only, with no heads, allowing thin filaments to slide past without obstruction
Heads are distributed along the length; the bare region is where calcium binds to regulate motor activity
Tails overlap; the bare region is where cross-bridges form most frequently
The filament is helical; the bare region is the internal hollow core
questions.explain: Myosin-II molecules self-assemble tail-to-tail with heads pointing outward at both ends. The central bare region (~0.2 µm) contains only tails; this allows antiparallel actin filaments to slide past each other without heads obstructing the motion.

questions.q: In sarcomeric contraction, myosin heads pull actin filaments toward the center. Which statement correctly describes what happens to the filament lengths?
options: Thick and thin filaments both shorten as their subunits are consumed
Thick and thin filaments shorten, but the subunits are not consumed
Both filament types maintain constant length; the sarcomere shortens as filaments slide past each other
Only the thin filaments shorten; thick filaments remain rigid
The plus ends of thin filaments retract toward the sarcomere center while minus ends depolymerize
questions.explain: The sliding filament model shows that myosin heads walk along actin, pulling thin filaments inward. Filament lengths do not change; instead, the overlap zone increases and the sarcomere shortens. This is a frequent source of confusion on exams.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between a muscle fiber and myofibrils?
options: Muscle fibers are organelles within myofibrils
Myofibrils are contractile bundles of sarcomeres running the length of the muscle fiber
Muscle fibers and myofibrils are separate structures that do not interact
Myofibrils connect muscle fibers to the extracellular matrix
Muscle fibers are formed by the fusion of multiple myofibrils
questions.explain: A muscle fiber (skeletal muscle cell) is multinucleate. It contains many myofibrils, which are cylindrical bundles of sarcomeres arranged in parallel. The striated appearance of muscle comes from the aligned myofibrils.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the Z disc in muscle?
options: It serves as the location of myosin-II motor protein heads
It anchors the plus ends of thin filaments from adjacent sarcomeres and defines the sarcomere boundary
It stores calcium for rapid release during contraction
It caps the minus ends of thick filaments
It serves as the insertion point for T tubules
questions.explain: Z discs are protein structures at each sarcomere boundary. They anchor the plus ends of thin filaments, which face the disc. The minus ends point toward the sarcomere center. Z discs move closer together during contraction, shortening the sarcomere.

questions.q: In the sarcomere, which region contains the overlap of thick and thin filaments where cross-bridges form?
options: The bare region in the center of the thick filament
The region immediately adjacent to each Z disc
The overlap region, where heads of myosin-II interact with thin filaments
The space between adjacent sarcomeres
The region where T tubules invaginate the plasma membrane
questions.explain: Cross-bridges form in the overlap region where thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments are interdigitated. The bare region at the thick filament center contains only tails and is devoid of heads.

questions.q: What is the structural relationship between T tubules and the sarcoplasmic reticulum?
options: They are continuous with each other; calcium flows freely between them
They are separated by a ~35 nm gap; voltage-gated channels in the T-tubule couple to calcium-release channels in the SR membrane
The SR is located inside T tubules
T tubules deliver calcium from the extracellular space directly into the SR lumen
They are completely independent structures with no functional interaction
questions.explain: T tubules and SR are closely apposed with a ~35 nm gap. DHPR (voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channel in T-tubule) is physically coupled to RyR (ryanodine receptor/Ca²⁺-release channel in SR). This mechanical coupling enables rapid, synchronous contraction.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the lumen of T tubules?
options: It is filled with calcium stored from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
It is a separate cytosolic compartment distinct from the main cell cytoplasm
It is continuous with the extracellular space; the action potential travels along the T-tubule membrane
It contains tropomyosin and troponin for local regulation
It is an extension of the nuclear envelope
questions.explain: T tubules are invaginations of the plasma membrane; their lumen is extracellular space, not cytosol. The action potential travels along the T-tubule membrane to trigger voltage-gated channels that mechanically open RyR channels in the adjacent SR. Calcium is released from the SR lumen into the cytosol.

questions.q: What is the first event in the excitation-contraction coupling cascade?
options: Calcium binds troponin C on the thin filament
Myosin heads bind to exposed actin-binding sites
The motor neuron fires and an action potential propagates along the muscle cell plasma membrane
Tropomyosin shifts laterally to expose myosin-binding sites
The sarcoplasmic reticulum releases calcium into the cytosol
questions.explain: The cascade begins when a motor neuron fires, triggering an action potential that propagates along the muscle cell membrane and down the T tubule. This voltage change is sensed by DHPR, which opens RyR to release calcium. Calcium then binds troponin C.

questions.q: How does calcium trigger the exposure of myosin-binding sites on actin?
options: Ca²⁺ directly binds to actin monomers, causing conformational change
Ca²⁺ binds troponin C, causing troponin I to release tropomyosin, which shifts ~1–1.5 nm laterally in the actin groove to expose binding sites
Ca²⁺ activates gelsolin to sever tropomyosin
Ca²⁺ dephosphorylates myosin light chains
Ca²⁺ binds directly to tropomyosin, causing it to dissociate from actin
questions.explain: In resting muscle, tropomyosin blocks myosin-binding sites on actin. Ca²⁺ binds troponin C (the calcium-sensing subunit). This causes troponin I (inhibitory subunit) to release tropomyosin, allowing it to shift and expose the binding sites.

questions.q: Which troponin subunit is responsible for binding calcium?
options: Troponin I
Troponin T
Troponin C
Troponin X
All three subunits bind calcium equally
questions.explain: Troponin C is the calcium-binding (calcium-sensing) subunit of the troponin complex. When Ca²⁺ binds, it triggers conformational changes in troponin I and troponin T, ultimately leading to tropomyosin displacement.

questions.q: What is the specific role of troponin I in muscle contraction?
options: It binds calcium to initiate the contraction signal
It anchors the entire troponin complex to tropomyosin along the filament
It holds tropomyosin in the blocking position in the absence of Ca²⁺; upon Ca²⁺ binding to troponin C, it releases tropomyosin
It hydrolyzes ATP to provide energy for contraction
It binds directly to myosin heads to prevent cross-bridge formation
questions.explain: Troponin I is the inhibitory subunit that holds tropomyosin in a blocking position. When troponin C binds Ca²⁺, it undergoes a conformational change that pulls troponin I away from tropomyosin, allowing tropomyosin to shift and expose myosin-binding sites.

questions.q: In the sliding filament model, what prevents myosin-actin cross-bridges from forming in resting (relaxed) muscle?
options: Calcium is absent, so myosin heads cannot bind ATP
Tropomyosin physically blocks the myosin-binding sites on actin; ATP is present but cross-bridges cannot form without calcium-triggered tropomyosin displacement
Myosin heads are bound to actin but in an inactive state
Gelsolin severs all actin filaments, preventing myosin access
The Z discs are locked in place by calcium-calmodulin complexes
questions.explain: In resting muscle, tropomyosin blocks myosin-binding sites on actin. Even though ATP is available, myosin heads cannot attach to actin. Only when Ca²⁺ rises and troponin C is occupied does tropomyosin shift to expose binding sites.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase during muscle relaxation?
options: It breaks down acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction
It pumps Ca²⁺ back into the SR lumen, lowering cytosolic [Ca²⁺] and allowing tropomyosin to re-cover myosin-binding sites
It hydrolyzes ATP to provide energy for thin filament shortening
It severs actin filaments to terminate contraction
It phosphorylates tropomyosin to enhance its blocking activity
questions.explain: During relaxation, SR Ca²⁺-ATPase pumps Ca²⁺ out of the cytosol back into the SR lumen. As [Ca²⁺] drops, Ca²⁺ dissociates from troponin C. Troponin reverts to its original conformation, tropomyosin re-covers myosin-binding sites, and cross-bridges cannot form.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT true about the three major cytoskeletal filaments?
options: Intermediate filaments provide mechanical strength but have no associated motor proteins
Microtubules are powered by kinesin and dynein motors
Actin filaments are the thickest of the three and are powered by myosin motors
All eukaryotic cells contain actin and microtubules, but not all contain intermediate filaments
The three filament systems collaborate to perform cellular functions
questions.explain: Actin filaments (~7 nm) are the thinnest, not the thickest. Microtubules (~25 nm) are thicker than actin. Intermediate filaments (~10 nm) are intermediate in size. Actin is indeed powered by myosin, but its diameter is the smallest of the three.

questions.q: Which statement correctly compares the subunits and nucleotides of the three major cytoskeletal filaments?
options: All three use the same subunit protein but different nucleotides
Intermediate filaments use keratins/lamins (no nucleotide); microtubules use tubulin (GTP); actin filaments use actin (ATP)
Microtubules and actin use the same nucleotide; intermediate filaments use a different one
All three use different proteins and different nucleotides, but the nucleotides are interchangeable
Intermediate filaments and actin both use ATP; microtubules use GTP
questions.explain: The three filaments differ in their subunits and nucleotide requirements. Intermediate filaments (keratins, lamins, vimentin) do not bind nucleotides. Microtubules use α/β-tubulin dimers with GTP. Actin filaments use G-actin monomers with ATP.

questions.q: What is the key functional difference between nucleation by formin versus nucleation by Arp2/3 in the context of cell morphology?
options: Formin produces structures that are wider and more stable; Arp2/3 produces thinner, more dynamic structures
Formin generates linear actin bundles (filopodia, stress fibers); Arp2/3 generates branched networks (lamellipodia) that are more flexible and form faster
Formin is activated by calcium; Arp2/3 is constitutively active
Formin produces thicker filaments; Arp2/3 produces thinner filaments
Formin is found only in migrating cells; Arp2/3 is found only in muscle
questions.explain: Formin nucleates linear bundles, producing structures like stress fibers and filopodia that are rigid and directional. Arp2/3 nucleates branched networks that form a dynamic dendritic mesh (lamellipodium). The branched architecture provides greater flexibility and faster assembly.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why the bare region in the myosin-II bipolar filament is functionally important?
options: It is where ATP binds to myosin heads to initiate the power stroke
It prevents the thick filament from shortening during contraction
It contains only tails, allowing antiparallel thin filaments to slide past without obstruction from myosin heads
It is the only region where troponin can bind
It serves as the attachment point for the Z disc
questions.explain: The bare region (~0.2 µm at the center) contains only myosin tails. This allows the two sets of antiparallel thin filaments to slide inward without being blocked by myosin heads, which are located only at the two ends of the bipolar filament.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the purpose of cell communication (細胞通訊) in multicellular organisms?
options: To eliminate waste products from the extracellular space
To directly transfer genetic material between neighboring cells
To maintain membrane potential by regulating ion concentrations
To recycle intracellular proteins through the proteasome pathway
To coordinate survival, growth, differentiation, and death through signal exchange
questions.explain: Cell communication allows cells to sense the environment, send orders to other cells, heal wounds, and guide developmental patterning — all requiring coordinated information exchange. Waste elimination, genetic transfer, membrane potential, and protein recycling are not the defining functions of cell-to-cell communication as a signaling framework.

questions.q: Yeast mating-type cells a and α secrete mating factors that cause the partner cell to elongate toward it before fusing. This is an example of which signaling phenomenon?
options: Contact-dependent signaling requiring direct membrane contact
Endocrine signaling carried through the yeast bloodstream
Synaptic signaling delivered by specialized nerve terminals
Autocrine signaling where cells respond to their own secreted factors
Signal transduction converting an extracellular chemical signal into a cellular response
questions.explain: Yeast mating involves secreted mating factors that bind receptors on the partner cell and trigger intracellular changes — a classic signal transduction event. Yeast lack a bloodstream (ruling out endocrine), the cells do not need to touch before responding (ruling out contact-dependent), and the signal targets the partner, not the secreting cell (ruling out autocrine).

questions.q: In contact-dependent signaling, what feature makes it distinct from all other modes of cell communication?
options: The signaling molecule is membrane-bound and requires direct cell-to-cell contact
The signal is secreted into the extracellular matrix and diffuses to nearby cells
The signal travels through the bloodstream to reach distant target cells
The signal is carried along an axon and released at a specialized terminal
The signal activates a G-protein-coupled receptor on the target cell surface
questions.explain: Contact-dependent signaling uses membrane-bound signal molecules that never leave the cell surface, so physical contact between the signaling and target cells is mandatory. All other modes (paracrine, endocrine, synaptic) involve secreted or released molecules that travel some distance through extracellular space or fluid.

questions.q: In the Delta–Notch signaling system, one epithelial cell expressing Delta activates Notch on all its neighbors. What is the functional outcome of this lateral inhibition?
options: All neighboring cells adopt the same specialized fate as the Delta-expressing cell
Neighboring cells are suppressed from adopting the same fate, producing cell specialization
The Delta-expressing cell is destroyed so that only Notch-bearing cells remain
Notch activation triggers apoptosis in all cells simultaneously
Delta is secreted and diffuses broadly to pattern the entire tissue layer
questions.explain: Lateral inhibition via Delta–Notch suppresses neighbors from adopting the same fate as the Delta-expressing cell, generating diversity from a field of equivalent cells — this is the mechanism of cell specialization. Delta remains membrane-bound and is not secreted, and Notch activation promotes differentiation into a different fate, not cell destruction.

questions.q: A student states: 'Paracrine and endocrine signaling both use secreted signal molecules, so the only difference is how far the signal travels.' Which part of this statement is correct, and what is the most important omission?
options: Both use secreted molecules — correct; but endocrine signals travel via the bloodstream while paracrine signals remain local — the route is the key distinction
The statement is fully correct; distance is the sole distinction between these modes
The statement is incorrect; paracrine signals are membrane-bound and endocrine signals are secreted
The statement is incorrect; endocrine signals are electrical and paracrine signals are chemical
Both use secreted molecules — correct; but paracrine signals enter the bloodstream whereas endocrine signals stay local
questions.explain: Paracrine signals (local mediators) are secreted but remain in the neighborhood of the secreting cell without entering the bloodstream; endocrine hormones are also secreted but travel via the bloodstream to distant targets. Distance and route through the bloodstream are both important distinctions — the student captured half the picture. Option E reverses which mode uses the bloodstream.

questions.q: Autocrine signaling is considered a variant of which broader signaling mode?
options: Contact-dependent signaling, because the signal never leaves the cell membrane
Endocrine signaling, because the hormone enters the bloodstream before re-binding
Synaptic signaling, because autocrine factors are released at specialized terminals
Intracellular signaling, because the signal molecule never crosses the plasma membrane
Paracrine signaling, because the cell secretes a local mediator and then responds to it itself
questions.explain: Autocrine signaling is a variant of paracrine signaling in which the same cell that secretes the local mediator also bears the receptor and responds to its own signal. Like paracrine, the signal is secreted and diffuses locally — the distinction is only that the source and target are the same cell.

questions.q: Insulin secreted by the pancreas travels through the bloodstream to cells throughout the body. Which property of endocrine signaling does this best illustrate?
options: High spatial precision — only cells immediately adjacent to the pancreas respond
Fast signal delivery — insulin reaches its target in milliseconds via the blood
Membrane-bound transmission — insulin remains attached to pancreatic cells during signaling
Broad reach — a single endocrine organ can simultaneously regulate many distant cell types
Synaptic precision — insulin is released only at nerve terminals adjacent to target cells
questions.explain: Endocrine signaling allows one secretory organ (the pancreas) to simultaneously affect many cell types throughout the body via the bloodstream — broad reach is the hallmark of this mode. Endocrine signaling is not spatially precise like synaptic signaling, is not fast like ion channel activation, and insulin is clearly secreted, not membrane-bound.

questions.q: A neuron releases neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft, where they bind receptors on the target cell. Which property distinguishes synaptic signaling from paracrine signaling?
options: Synaptic signals are secreted molecules, while paracrine signals are membrane-bound
Paracrine signaling uses electrical impulses, while synaptic signaling uses chemical transmitters
Synaptic signaling requires the signal to enter the bloodstream for delivery to target cells
Synaptic signaling is spatially directed through specialized nerve terminals — like private lines — whereas paracrine signals diffuse broadly in the tissue
Paracrine signaling operates only in neural tissue, while synaptic signaling operates in all tissues
questions.explain: Synaptic signaling is delivered with high spatial precision through the nerve terminal directly to the synapse — neurotransmitters cross a narrow cleft to a specific target cell. Paracrine signals diffuse more broadly in the extracellular space. Both use chemical messengers, and neither requires entry into the bloodstream.

questions.q: Which sequence correctly describes the three phases of signal transduction in order?
options: Response → Transduction → Reception
Transduction → Reception → Response
Reception → Response → Transduction
Reception → Transduction → Response
Amplification → Reception → Response
questions.explain: The canonical order is Reception (the receptor detects the extracellular signal), then Transduction (the signal is converted and relayed through intracellular cascades), then Response (the cellular outcome — altered metabolism, shape, or gene expression). All other orderings break the logical flow of extracellular → intracellular → output.

questions.q: A signaling pathway converts receptor activation into altered gene expression. Which phase of signal transduction includes the functions of relay, amplification, integration, distribution, and feedback control?
options: Reception, because the receptor coordinates all downstream events
Response, because it encompasses all changes in cell behavior
Secretion, because the ligand must be processed before it can relay information
Elimination, because signal inactivation is required before any response occurs
Transduction, because it converts and processes the signal through intracellular cascades
questions.explain: Transduction is the phase that converts the extracellular signal into an intracellular form capable of driving a response; it encompasses relay, amplification (one signal activates many downstream molecules), integration of multiple inputs, distribution to multiple effectors, and feedback control. Reception is only the binding event; response is the final output.

questions.q: Which type of signal molecule uses a cell-surface receptor rather than an intracellular receptor, and why?
options: Small hydrophobic molecules, because they require a receptor embedded in the membrane to cross it
Large hydrophilic molecules, because they cannot cross the hydrophobic lipid bilayer and bind receptors on the cell surface
Steroid hormones, because they are too large to be detected by intracellular machinery
Gas-phase signals, because they bind receptors on the extracellular face of the membrane
All signal molecules, because every signal requires an initial interaction with a cell-surface protein
questions.explain: Large, charged, or hydrophilic signal molecules cannot diffuse through the hydrophobic lipid bilayer, so they bind receptors anchored on the cell surface that transmit the signal inside. Small hydrophobic molecules (e.g., steroid hormones) cross the membrane freely and bind intracellular receptors.

questions.q: A researcher describes an intracellular receptor as 'extracellular' because 'the signal comes from outside the cell.' What is the error in this reasoning?
options: There is no error — any receptor that detects an external signal is extracellular by definition
The error is that intracellular receptors are always located on the plasma membrane, not inside the cell
The error is conflating the receptor's location with the signal's origin — the receptor itself is inside the cell even though the signal molecule originates outside
The error is that intracellular receptors only detect signals synthesized within the cytosol
The error is that extracellular signals always bind cell-surface receptors, making intracellular receptors impossible
questions.explain: The term 'intracellular receptor' refers to where the receptor protein resides — inside the cell (cytosol or nucleus). The signal molecule (e.g., cortisol) does originate outside but crosses the membrane before binding its intracellular receptor. Location of the receptor and origin of the signal are independent facts.

questions.q: Cortisol is a steroid hormone. What property allows it to reach its receptor without a cell-surface receptor?
options: Cortisol is actively transported into the cell by a dedicated membrane pump
Cortisol binds a channel protein that opens and allows it to enter
Cortisol is hydrophobic and freely diffuses through the lipid bilayer of the plasma membrane
Cortisol is broken down at the membrane and reconstructed inside the cell
Cortisol enters through gap junctions shared between adjacent cells
questions.explain: Cortisol is a steroid hormone with a hydrophobic structure that allows it to dissolve in and diffuse through the lipid bilayer without the assistance of a transporter or channel. This is what distinguishes steroid hormones from large hydrophilic signals that need cell-surface receptors.

questions.q: Where is the cortisol nuclear receptor located BEFORE cortisol binds to it?
options: On the outer surface of the plasma membrane
Inside the nucleus, already bound to DNA regulatory regions
In the cytosol, in an inactive conformation
In the endoplasmic reticulum, awaiting ligand-induced secretion
In the lysosome, protected from premature activation
questions.explain: Before cortisol binds, the nuclear receptor protein resides in the cytosol in an inactive conformation. Cortisol binding causes a conformational change that activates the receptor, and only then does the receptor-cortisol complex translocate into the nucleus to bind DNA regulatory regions. Students commonly but incorrectly assume the receptor starts in the nucleus.

questions.q: Place the following steps of cortisol signaling in the correct order: (1) receptor-cortisol complex binds DNA regulatory region; (2) cortisol crosses the plasma membrane; (3) conformational change activates receptor; (4) complex translocates to nucleus; (5) cortisol binds cytosolic nuclear receptor.
questions.explain: Cortisol first crosses the plasma membrane (2) due to its hydrophobic nature, then binds the cytosolic nuclear receptor (5), triggering a conformational change that activates it (3), after which the active complex translocates to the nucleus (4), and finally binds the DNA regulatory region to activate transcription (1). This ordered sequence is the canonical cortisol signaling pathway.

questions.q: Which of the three cell-surface receptor classes directly controls ion flow as an immediate consequence of ligand binding?
options: G-protein-coupled receptors, because they activate a G protein that opens separate ion channels
Enzyme-coupled receptors, because receptor dimerization creates a pore in the membrane
Ion-channel-coupled receptors, because ligand binding opens the channel built into the receptor protein itself
Intracellular receptors, because they translocate to the nucleus and activate ion transporter genes
Receptor tyrosine kinases, because phosphorylation of the receptor creates an ion-conducting pathway
questions.explain: Ion-channel-coupled receptors contain the ion channel as an integral part of the receptor protein — ligand binding causes a conformational change that opens the pore, allowing ions to flow immediately (within milliseconds). GPCRs activate G proteins that may indirectly regulate separate channels; enzyme-coupled receptors activate kinase domains, not ion pores.

questions.q: A GPCR is activated by its ligand. Which molecular event directly converts the G protein from its inactive to its active state?
options: The activated receptor phosphorylates the G protein on a tyrosine residue
The G protein subunit exchanges GDP for GTP, releasing the active subunit to trigger downstream effectors
The G protein cleaves GTP to GDP, releasing energy to drive conformational change
The receptor dimerizes, allowing two G proteins to simultaneously bind and cross-activate each other
The G protein translocates to the nucleus and binds a DNA regulatory element
questions.explain: Activated GPCRs catalyze exchange of GDP for GTP on the G protein alpha subunit; the GTP-bound active subunit then dissociates and triggers downstream effectors. GTP hydrolysis (not an activating event but the inactivation step) returns the G protein to its OFF state. GPCRs have no intrinsic kinase activity and do not phosphorylate the G protein.

questions.q: Enzyme-coupled receptors are often activated by a dimeric signal molecule. Why does dimerization of the signal matter?
options: A dimeric signal doubles the amount of ligand, increasing receptor occupancy by mass action
A dimeric signal cross-links two receptor monomers, bringing their cytoplasmic catalytic domains into proximity to activate each other
A dimeric signal prevents receptor internalization, prolonging signaling duration
A dimeric signal activates the receptor's G protein by stabilizing the GDP-bound conformation
A dimeric signal allows the receptor to form an ion channel by bridging two membrane leaflets
questions.explain: Enzyme-coupled receptors (e.g., receptor tyrosine kinases) require dimerization — a dimeric signal molecule simultaneously binds two receptor monomers, cross-linking them and bringing their cytoplasmic catalytic (kinase) domains into proximity so they can transphosphorylate and activate each other. This structural requirement explains why a monomeric signal cannot activate the receptor.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about cell-surface receptor classes is NOT correct?
options: Ion-channel-coupled receptors operate on a millisecond timescale after ligand binding
GPCRs contain seven transmembrane helices
GPCRs activate intrinsic tyrosine kinase domains upon ligand binding
Enzyme-coupled receptors include receptor tyrosine kinases
Enzyme-coupled receptors are activated by signal-induced dimerization of receptor monomers
questions.explain: GPCRs do NOT have intrinsic kinase activity — their signaling mechanism relies on activating trimeric G proteins, not phosphorylating targets directly. Intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity belongs to enzyme-coupled receptors (receptor tyrosine kinases). All other statements accurately describe the respective receptor classes.

questions.q: Protein kinase and protein phosphatase are both enzymes that regulate signaling proteins. Which statement correctly describes their opposing roles in the phosphorylation switch?
options: Protein kinase adds a phosphate group from ATP (turns signaling ON); protein phosphatase removes the phosphate (turns signaling OFF)
Protein kinase removes a phosphate group (turns signaling ON); protein phosphatase adds a phosphate group (turns signaling OFF)
Both protein kinase and phosphatase add phosphate groups, but to different residues
Protein kinase hydrolyzes GTP to GDP to activate signaling; phosphatase reverses this by adding GTP
Protein kinase activates signaling by cleaving the substrate protein; phosphatase restores it by ligating the fragments
questions.explain: Protein kinase transfers a phosphate group from ATP to the target protein, switching it ON; protein phosphatase catalyzes hydrolytic removal of that phosphate, switching the protein OFF and returning it to its basal state. This reversible modification is one of the two main molecular switch mechanisms in signaling cascades.

questions.q: Ras is a canonical GTP-binding switch protein. In its active state, what molecular event eventually returns Ras to its OFF (inactive) state?
options: A protein phosphatase removes a phosphate from Ras, releasing GDP
A protein kinase phosphorylates Ras on its catalytic arginine, blocking GTP binding
GDP spontaneously dissociates from Ras, leaving an empty nucleotide-binding pocket
A guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) re-loads Ras with GDP to inactivate it
Ras intrinsic GTPase activity hydrolyzes GTP to GDP plus inorganic phosphate
questions.explain: Active (GTP-bound) Ras is returned to its OFF state by its intrinsic GTPase activity, which slowly hydrolyzes GTP to GDP + Pᵢ; the GDP-bound form is inactive. This built-in timer is important for signal termination. GEFs actually promote GDP release and GTP loading (activation), not inactivation.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes the two molecular switch mechanisms used in intracellular signaling?
options: Phosphorylation switch: kinase adds GTP; GTP-binding switch: GTPase adds phosphate
Phosphorylation switch: kinase ON / phosphatase OFF; GTP-binding switch: GTP binding ON / GTP hydrolysis OFF
Phosphorylation switch: reversible; GTP-binding switch: irreversible once GTP is bound
Both switches use the same mechanism but differ only in which downstream pathway they activate
Phosphorylation switch: uses GDP/GTP exchange; GTP-binding switch: uses ATP/ADP phosphorylation
questions.explain: The phosphorylation switch is driven by protein kinase (adds phosphate from ATP → ON) and protein phosphatase (removes phosphate → OFF). The GTP-binding switch is driven by GTP binding (ON) and intrinsic GTPase-catalyzed hydrolysis to GDP (OFF). These are mechanistically distinct and must not be conflated.

questions.q: In a signaling cascade, activating one receptor eventually activates thousands of intracellular effector molecules. Which function of intracellular signaling pathways does this best describe?
options: Integration — multiple input signals are merged at a single protein
Distribution — one receptor branches to activate multiple parallel pathways
Amplification — each step activates many more downstream molecules, exponentially increasing signal strength
Feedback control — downstream products inhibit upstream components to limit the response
Relay — the signal is passed sequentially from one protein to the next
questions.explain: Amplification describes the cascade property whereby one activated receptor triggers many molecules at the next step, each of which activates many more — producing an exponential increase (domino effect) so that a single receptor activation event can yield thousands of downstream molecular events. Relay is just sequential passing; distribution involves branching, not numerical expansion.

questions.q: A cell simultaneously receives signals from three different receptors. These signals converge and are processed together at a single intracellular signaling protein before a response is committed. Which pathway function does this illustrate?
options: Amplification, because each of the three signals multiplies the total signal strength
Relay, because each signal passes through the shared protein in sequence
Feedback control, because three inputs compete to inhibit each other
Integration, because multiple concurrent inputs from different pathways are merged at one protein
Distribution, because one receptor splits its signal to three downstream branches
questions.explain: Integration is the pathway function where inputs from multiple signaling pathways converge at a single protein so the cell can sense combinations of signals rather than each in isolation. This allows nuanced decision-making (e.g., survive vs. apoptose). Distribution is the inverse — one receptor sending to multiple branches.

questions.q: Acetylcholine causes decreased firing rate in heart pacemaker cells, secretion in salivary gland cells, and contraction in skeletal muscle cells. What does this phenomenon demonstrate?
options: Acetylcholine is three different molecules with different structures in each tissue
Acetylcholine must be modified after secretion to acquire tissue-specific activity
Different cell types have different signal-transduction phases — some lack transduction entirely
The same signal molecule can produce different responses depending on which receptors and signaling pathways are present in the target cell
Acetylcholine binds only one receptor type but that receptor produces different ligands in each tissue
questions.explain: The acetylcholine example classically illustrates that it is not the signal molecule itself but the receptor identity and downstream signaling machinery of the receiving cell that determines the response. Acetylcholine is structurally identical in all cases; the heart, salivary gland, and muscle cells express different receptor subtypes and pathway components.

questions.q: Muscle contraction occurs within seconds of nerve stimulation, while cell differentiation requires hours. What is the mechanistic basis for this difference in response speed?
options: Fast responses require new protein synthesis; slow responses use pre-existing proteins
Fast responses require gene expression changes; slow responses use pre-existing protein activation
Fast responses alter the function of pre-existing proteins; slow responses require new protein synthesis via altered gene expression
