Fast responses use paracrine signals; slow responses use endocrine hormones
Fast responses are driven by endocrine signaling; slow responses by contact-dependent signaling
questions.explain: Fast responses (seconds to minutes) alter pre-existing protein function directly — no gene expression or new synthesis needed, just activation of dormant machinery (e.g., opening ion channels or activating existing enzymes). Slow responses (minutes to hours) require the signal to alter transcription so new proteins can be synthesized. Options A and B reverse the relationship.

questions.q: Which of the following is the primary function of YAP and TAZ in the HIPPO pathway?
options: To phosphorylate DCH S1/2 and FAT4 receptors to activate the kinase cascade
To sequester 14-3-3 proteins in the cytoplasm, preventing their nuclear entry
To act as transcriptional co-activators in the nucleus, driving expression of pro-growth genes
To hydrolyze GTP on Ras-like proteins, terminating the proliferation signal
To form ion channels that allow Ca²⁺ entry and trigger downstream kinase activation
questions.explain: YAP and TAZ are transcriptional co-activators; when unphosphorylated they enter the nucleus and drive expression of pro-growth transcription factors, promoting cell proliferation. 14-3-3 proteins sequester phosphorylated YAP/TAZ in the cytoplasm (not the other way around), and YAP/TAZ have no GTPase or ion channel function.

questions.q: At high cell density, how does the HIPPO pathway prevent cell proliferation?
options: Cell-cell contacts inactivate DCH S1/2 and FAT4, allowing YAP/TAZ to enter the nucleus freely
Cell-cell contacts activate DCH S1/2 and FAT4, triggering a kinase cascade that phosphorylates YAP/TAZ, which are then retained in the cytoplasm by 14-3-3 proteins
High density directly inhibits transcription factor binding to pro-growth gene promoters without requiring kinase activity
YAP/TAZ phosphorylation at high density promotes their nuclear import and activation of apoptotic genes
High cell density saturates extracellular growth factor receptors, indirectly activating HIPPO by depleting available ligand
questions.explain: At high cell density, membrane-bound regulators DCH S1/2 and FAT4 are engaged by cell-cell contacts, activating a downstream kinase cascade that phosphorylates YAP/TAZ. Phosphorylated YAP/TAZ are recognized and sequestered in the cytoplasm by 14-3-3 proteins, preventing nuclear entry and blocking pro-growth gene expression — implementing contact inhibition.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about the mouse liver HIPPO experiment (Dong et al., Cell, 2007) is NOT correct?
options: Inducible expression of activated YAP was triggered using doxycycline
Liver/body weight ratio increased from approximately 5% to approximately 17% within two weeks
The liver overgrowth was reversible upon turning off the YAP transgene
Tumors developed within one week of transgene induction, demonstrating rapid oncogenesis
At eight weeks of continuous YAP activity, tumors developed in the liver
questions.explain: Tumors appeared at eight weeks, not within one week — the overgrowth over the first two weeks was reversible hyperplasia, not immediate tumor formation. Statements A, B, C, and E accurately describe the experimental data from Dong et al. The one-week claim in option D is a fabricated timeline that contradicts the published findings.

questions.q: A loss-of-function mutation in HIPPO pathway kinases (that normally phosphorylate YAP/TAZ) would be predicted to cause which outcome?
options: Constitutive YAP/TAZ inactivation and arrest of cell proliferation in all tissues
Constitutive nuclear localization of unphosphorylated YAP/TAZ and excessive cell proliferation or organ overgrowth
Increased 14-3-3-mediated cytoplasmic sequestration of YAP/TAZ, reducing pro-growth signaling
Enhanced activation of DCH S1/2 and FAT4 leading to accelerated contact inhibition
Selective apoptosis of high-density cell populations due to failure of the survival signal
questions.explain: If HIPPO kinases cannot phosphorylate YAP/TAZ, then YAP/TAZ remain unphosphorylated regardless of cell density and constitutively enter the nucleus to drive pro-growth gene expression — mimicking the low-density state even in crowded tissue. This leads to uncontrolled proliferation and organ overgrowth, consistent with the fruit fly wing disc data showing massive overgrowth when HIPPO signaling is disrupted.

questions.q: Which structural feature correctly describes G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs)?
options: Single-pass transmembrane protein with an intracellular kinase domain
Double-pass transmembrane protein with two membrane-spanning helices
Seven-pass transmembrane protein with seven membrane-spanning helices
Peripheral membrane protein anchored by a GPI lipid tail
Soluble nuclear receptor that binds lipid-soluble ligands in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: GPCRs are defined by their seven-pass (heptahelical) transmembrane topology. The most common error is confusing GPCRs with RTKs, which are single-pass transmembrane proteins; single-pass is the description of enzyme-coupled receptors, not GPCRs.

questions.q: Approximately what fraction of currently used drugs exert their effects through GPCRs?
options: ~5% of currently used drugs
~10% of currently used drugs
~20% of currently used drugs
~30% of currently used drugs
~50% of currently used drugs
questions.explain: Approximately 30% of all drugs in clinical use act through GPCRs, reflecting the enormous therapeutic importance of this receptor family. The ~400 olfactory receptor figure is a different statistic about the GPCR family size, not about drug targets.

questions.q: When a ligand binds an active GPCR and GDP dissociates from the Gα subunit, what event immediately follows?
options: Gα is phosphorylated by a receptor-associated kinase and becomes active
GTP binds Gα, causing Gα-GTP to dissociate from Gβγ
Gβγ hydrolyzes GTP to GDP and reassociates with Gα
The receptor internalizes and Gα is degraded by the proteasome
IP₃ is released from the membrane and diffuses to the ER
questions.explain: GDP dissociation creates an empty nucleotide-binding site on Gα; GTP (which is present at high concentrations in the cytosol) rapidly fills this site, producing Gα-GTP, which then dissociates from Gβγ. GTP hydrolysis (not GTP binding) is the inactivating step that comes later.

questions.q: What molecular event terminates the activity of Gα-GTP signaling?
options: A cytosolic phosphatase removes the phosphate from Gα
Gβγ binds back to Gα and blocks its effector-binding surface
The intrinsic GTPase activity of Gα hydrolyzes GTP to GDP
The ligand dissociates from the GPCR and allows Gα to re-associate
Ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of Gα-GTP
questions.explain: The duration of Gα signaling is determined by the intrinsic GTPase (GTP-hydrolyzing) activity built into the Gα subunit itself. Once GTP is hydrolyzed to GDP, Gα returns to its inactive conformation and reassociates with Gβγ. Phosphatases and protein degradation are not the primary mechanism.

questions.q: After a GPCR ligand triggers G protein dissociation, which statement correctly describes the activity of the resulting subunits?
options: Only Gα-GTP is active; Gβγ remains inactive until Gα reassociates
Only Gβγ is active; Gα-GTP is sequestered by a chaperone protein
Both Gα-GTP and free Gβγ are active and each can activate distinct effectors
Neither subunit is active until the receptor is phosphorylated by GRK
Gα-GTP and Gβγ form a new heterotrimer with a different Gα isoform
questions.explain: Both Gα-GTP and free Gβγ are active effector-activating units after dissociation. For example, Gβγ directly opens K⁺ channels in heart pacemaker cells. Students frequently attribute all downstream activity only to Gα, missing the important role of Gβγ.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a direct effector activated by heterotrimeric G proteins downstream of a GPCR?
options: Adenylyl cyclase, producing cAMP from ATP
Phospholipase C, cleaving PIP₂ into IP₃ and DAG
Ion channels, such as K⁺ channels in cardiac pacemaker cells
Receptor tyrosine kinases, triggering phosphotyrosine docking
None of the above — all four are direct G-protein effectors
questions.explain: The three canonical G-protein effectors are adenylyl cyclase, phospholipase C, and ion channels. RTKs are a completely separate class of cell-surface receptors activated by growth factors, not by GPCR-coupled G proteins.

questions.q: In heart pacemaker cells, which subunit of the G protein directly opens K⁺ channels to slow heart rate after acetylcholine binds its muscarinic GPCR?
options: Gα-GDP, the inactive form of the alpha subunit
Gα-GTP, the active form of the alpha subunit
Gβγ, the free beta-gamma complex after G protein dissociation
The GPCR itself, which changes conformation to gate the channel
cAMP, generated by adenylyl cyclase after G protein activation
questions.explain: In heart pacemaker cells, the free Gβγ complex directly opens K⁺ channels, causing K⁺ efflux, hyperpolarization, and slowed heart rate. This is a key example of Gβγ-mediated effector activation, not Gα-mediated, which is a common source of confusion.

questions.q: Adrenaline binds its adrenergic GPCR, activates the Gs protein, and ultimately increases glycogen breakdown in muscle. Which intracellular relay correctly describes this pathway?
options: Gs → phospholipase C → IP₃ → Ca²⁺ → PKC → glycogen breakdown
Gs → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → glycogen breakdown
Gs → PI3-kinase → PIP₃ → Akt → glycogen breakdown
Gs → Ras-GEF → Ras-GTP → MAPK → glycogen breakdown
Gs → guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → PKG → glycogen breakdown
questions.explain: The Gs/adenylyl cyclase/cAMP/PKA pathway is the canonical route through which adrenaline stimulates glycogen breakdown. The PLC/PKC and PI3K/Akt pathways are distinct and not involved in this adrenaline response.

questions.q: Which second messenger directly activates Protein Kinase A (PKA)?
options: Diacylglycerol (DAG) produced by PLC
Inositol trisphosphate (IP₃) released into the cytosol
Cyclic AMP (cAMP) synthesized by adenylyl cyclase
Calcium ions released from the endoplasmic reticulum
Phosphatidylinositol trisphosphate (PIP₃) in the inner leaflet
questions.explain: cAMP binds to the regulatory subunits of PKA, releasing the catalytic subunits and activating them. DAG and Ca²⁺ together activate PKC, not PKA — confusing these two pathways and their respective kinases is one of the most common exam errors.

questions.q: Phospholipase C (PLC) is activated by a G protein. Which pair of molecules does it generate by cleaving PIP₂?
options: cAMP and cGMP, two cyclic nucleotide second messengers
IP₃ and diacylglycerol (DAG), two distinct second messengers
PIP₃ and PDK1, a lipid second messenger and a kinase
GDP and GTP, exchanged on the Gα subunit
Arachidonic acid and lysophospholipid, prostaglandin precursors
questions.explain: PLC cleaves the membrane phospholipid PIP₂ into IP₃ (water-soluble, diffuses to ER) and DAG (lipid-soluble, stays in membrane). Both products are second messengers. Students often name only one — the exam expects both.

questions.q: After PLC cleaves PIP₂, IP₃ and DAG are generated. What is the primary initial source of the Ca²⁺ that is released into the cytosol?
options: Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels in the plasma membrane open in response to IP₃
IP₃ opens Ca²⁺ channels on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane
DAG chelates extracellular Ca²⁺ and transports it across the plasma membrane
Mitochondria release stored Ca²⁺ in response to elevated DAG
Adenylyl cyclase-generated cAMP opens ER Ca²⁺ channels indirectly
questions.explain: IP₃ (inositol trisphosphate) is water-soluble and diffuses to the ER membrane, where it binds and opens IP₃-gated Ca²⁺ channels, releasing Ca²⁺ from the ER lumen into the cytosol. The ER Ca²⁺ concentration (~1 mM) is far higher than resting cytosolic levels (~10⁻⁴ mM), driving rapid release.

questions.q: A sperm fertilizes an egg and a Ca²⁺ wave sweeps across the egg. Which signaling molecules are responsible for this Ca²⁺ release?
options: cAMP activates PKA, which phosphorylates ER Ca²⁺ pumps to open them
IP₃ opens Ca²⁺ channels on the ER, releasing stored Ca²⁺ into the cytosol
PIP₃ recruits Akt, which phosphorylates and opens plasma membrane Ca²⁺ channels
Ras-GTP activates MAPK, which phosphorylates Ca²⁺ transporters
Free Gβγ directly opens voltage-gated plasma membrane Ca²⁺ channels
questions.explain: The sperm-triggered Ca²⁺ wave in an egg is a classic example of IP₃-mediated Ca²⁺ release from ER stores. PLC generates IP₃ from PIP₂, IP₃ opens ER Ca²⁺ channels, and Ca²⁺ floods the cytosol, propagating as a wave. This is the PLC/Ca²⁺ pathway, not the cAMP/PKA or PI3K/Akt pathways.

questions.q: Which description correctly contrasts enzyme-coupled receptors with GPCRs?
options: Enzyme-coupled receptors have seven transmembrane helices; GPCRs have one
Enzyme-coupled receptors are single-pass transmembrane proteins; GPCRs have seven passes
Enzyme-coupled receptors couple exclusively to Gβγ; GPCRs couple to Gα only
Enzyme-coupled receptors act exclusively in the nucleus; GPCRs act at the plasma membrane
Enzyme-coupled receptors use cAMP as a second messenger; GPCRs use IP₃ only
questions.explain: Enzyme-coupled receptors (including RTKs) are single-pass transmembrane proteins, whereas GPCRs are seven-pass. Mixing up these transmembrane topologies is one of the most frequently tested exam traps in cell signaling.

questions.q: Enzyme-coupled receptors primarily respond to which class of extracellular ligands, and what downstream outcome do they typically regulate?
options: Neurotransmitters that regulate ion channel gating and membrane potential
Steroid hormones that bind intracellular receptors and directly activate gene transcription
Growth factors that regulate cell growth, proliferation, differentiation, and survival via gene expression changes
Second messengers such as cAMP that amplify signals within the same cell
Odorant molecules that activate olfactory neurons via GPCR cascades
questions.explain: Enzyme-coupled receptors respond primarily to growth factors and their activation typically leads to changes in gene expression that regulate proliferation, differentiation, growth, and survival. They are distinct from GPCRs (which handle neurotransmitters and hormones) and steroid receptors (which are intracellular).

questions.q: Which three subtypes correctly represent the major classes of enzyme-coupled receptors?
options: Ion channel receptors, GPCR-coupled receptors, and nuclear receptors
cAMP-linked receptors, cGMP-linked receptors, and IP₃-linked receptors
Tyrosine kinase receptors, serine/threonine kinase receptors, and guanylyl cyclase-linked receptors
Tyrosine kinase receptors, lipid kinase receptors, and phosphatase-linked receptors
MAPK-linked receptors, PI3K-linked receptors, and PKC-linked receptors
questions.explain: The three main subtypes of enzyme-coupled receptors are tyrosine kinase receptors (RTKs), serine/threonine kinase receptors (e.g., TGF-β receptors), and guanylyl cyclase-linked receptors. Knowing all three is important — listing only two is a common exam shortfall.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of events when an RTK is activated by a dimeric growth factor ligand?
options: Receptor dimerizes → ligand binds → transautophosphorylation → docking site formation
Ligand binds → receptor dimerizes → transautophosphorylation of tyrosines → phosphotyrosine docking sites assembled
Ligand binds → cAMP rises → PKA is activated → tyrosines are phosphorylated
Receptor dimerizes spontaneously → ligand stabilizes the dimer → GDP dissociates from Gα
Ligand binds → PI3-kinase phosphorylates the receptor tail → Akt docks directly on RTK
questions.explain: Ligand binding is the triggering event that causes RTK dimerization; the receptors do not pre-dimerize. Dimerization activates the intracellular kinase domains, which then transautophosphorylate tyrosine residues on each other's cytoplasmic tails, creating docking sites for downstream signaling proteins.

questions.q: Each phosphotyrosine on an activated RTK tail serves as a docking site. Which statement about these docking sites is correct?
options: All phosphotyrosines recruit the same adaptor protein, ensuring signal uniformity
Each phosphotyrosine recruits a different specific signaling protein, enabling multiple parallel pathways
Phosphotyrosines are recognized by SH2-domain proteins only if GDP is present on Ras
Docking sites are formed before dimerization to prime the receptor for activation
Phosphotyrosines recruit only serine kinases that then phosphorylate cytoskeletal proteins
questions.explain: A key feature of RTK signaling is pathway multiplexing: each phosphotyrosine sequence is recognized by a different intracellular signaling protein, allowing one activated RTK to simultaneously activate multiple distinct downstream cascades (e.g., both the Ras/MAPK and PI3K/Akt pathways).

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a correct statement about RTK activation?
options: Ligand binding induces dimerization of two RTK monomers
Dimerization activates the intracellular kinase domain of each receptor
The kinase domain phosphorylates tyrosine residues on the adjacent receptor tail
RTK monomers spontaneously dimerize in the absence of ligand to pre-activate the receptor
Multiple phosphotyrosines on the activated RTK create simultaneous parallel signaling
questions.explain: RTKs do NOT spontaneously dimerize in the absence of ligand; ligand binding is the event that induces dimerization. Constitutive (ligand-independent) dimerization would lead to uncontrolled, cancer-driving signaling — which is why some EGFR mutations that cause constitutive activity are oncogenic.

questions.q: A researcher mutates tyrosine Y3 of an RTK to alanine. They observe that signaling protein X no longer co-immunoprecipitates with the activated receptor. What conclusion is correct?
options: Phospho-Y3 is the specific docking site that recruits signaling protein X
Y3 is required for GDP–GTP exchange on the Gα subunit of the associated G protein
Y3 phosphorylation is needed to activate adenylyl cyclase downstream of the RTK
Alanine at position 3 causes receptor misfolding and prevents all downstream signaling
Phospho-Y3 recruits Gβγ to the RTK cytoplasmic tail to amplify signal
questions.explain: Mutating a tyrosine to alanine prevents phosphorylation at that site. If a specific signaling protein no longer associates with the receptor after this mutation, it means phospho-Y3 was the docking site for that protein. This tyrosine-to-alanine mutagenesis strategy is the standard experimental method for mapping RTK docking sites.

questions.q: A researcher expresses a constitutively active (GTP-locked) form of Ras in cells that lack protein Y. These cells signal normally through the MAPK pathway. What does this result indicate about protein Y?
options: Protein Y acts downstream of Ras in the MAPK cascade
Protein Y acts upstream of Ras, because bypassing Ras rescues signaling without Y
Protein Y is the same as Ras-GEF (SOS) and is required for RTK signaling only
Protein Y acts in a completely separate pathway unrelated to Ras or MAPK
Protein Y is a tumor suppressor that inhibits Ras-GTP loading
questions.explain: If constitutively active Ras rescues MAPK signaling in cells lacking protein Y, then Y must act upstream of Ras — because bypassing the Ras-activation step is sufficient to restore the pathway. If Y were downstream of Ras, constitutively active Ras would not rescue the defect.

questions.q: What is the role of Grb2 in the RTK → Ras signaling cascade?
options: Grb2 acts as a Ras-GEF and directly promotes GDP-to-GTP exchange on Ras
Grb2 acts as an adaptor protein: it docks on a phosphotyrosine of the RTK and recruits SOS (Ras-GEF)
Grb2 directly activates adenylyl cyclase to produce cAMP downstream of RTK
Grb2 is a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that turns off Ras after signal transmission
Grb2 phosphorylates Raf to initiate the MAP kinase cascade
questions.explain: Grb2 is an adaptor protein that lacks catalytic activity. It docks on an RTK phosphotyrosine via its SH2 domain and simultaneously recruits SOS (the Ras-GEF) via its SH3 domain. SOS is the molecule that actually promotes nucleotide exchange on Ras — Grb2 just bridges RTK to SOS.

questions.q: Which correctly describes the function of a Ras-GEF (guanine nucleotide exchange factor) such as SOS?
options: It phosphorylates Ras on serine residues to activate it
It hydrolyzes GTP on Ras to inactivate it and terminate signaling
It promotes dissociation of GDP from Ras and loading of GTP, thereby activating Ras
It anchors Ras to the plasma membrane by adding a lipid modification
It ubiquitinates Ras to target it for proteasomal degradation after signaling
questions.explain: GEF stands for guanine nucleotide exchange factor — it catalyzes the exchange of GDP for GTP on Ras, activating it. This is not phosphorylation (a common wrong answer). GTP hydrolysis is the inactivating step, carried out by Ras's own intrinsic GTPase activity (analogous to Gα).

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of human cancers contain activating mutations in Ras?
options: ~5% of human cancers
~10% of human cancers
~20% of human cancers
~30% of human cancers
~50% of human cancers
questions.explain: Approximately 30% of human cancers harbor activating mutations in Ras that lock it in a constitutively GTP-bound state, continuously driving proliferation without upstream signals. This same 30% figure also applies to drugs acting through GPCRs, but the Ras cancer statistic is a distinct exam-relevant fact.

questions.q: In the RTK → MAP kinase cascade, what is the correct order of signal transducers downstream of active Ras?
options: Ras → MEK → Raf → MAPK → nucleus
Ras → Raf → MEK → MAPK → nucleus
Ras → MAPK → MEK → Raf → nucleus
Ras → PI3K → PDK1 → Akt → nucleus
Ras → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → nucleus
questions.explain: The MAP kinase cascade downstream of Ras proceeds: Ras → Raf (a kinase) → MEK (a kinase) → MAPK → nucleus, where it changes gene expression. The PI3K/Akt branch is a separate RTK downstream pathway and should not be conflated with the Ras/MAPK cascade.

questions.q: What molecule does PI3-kinase directly phosphorylate in the RTK → PI3K → Akt pathway?
options: The Akt protein, activating it directly by phosphorylating its activation loop
PDK1, which then recruits Akt to the plasma membrane
An inositol phospholipid (PIP₂) in the inner membrane leaflet, generating PIP₃
The cytoplasmic tail of the RTK, creating additional docking sites
Ras, converting it from its GDP-bound to GTP-bound active state
questions.explain: PI3-kinase is a lipid kinase — it phosphorylates the 3-position of the inositol ring on PIP₂ to generate PIP₃. PIP₃ is a lipid second messenger that serves as a membrane docking site for PDK1 and Akt. PI3-kinase does not phosphorylate proteins directly.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT correct regarding the PI3K/Akt pathway?
options: PI3-kinase generates PIP₃ by phosphorylating a membrane lipid
PIP₃ recruits both PDK1 and Akt to the inner plasma membrane
PDK1 phosphorylates and activates Akt
PTEN opposes PI3-kinase by dephosphorylating PIP₃ back to PIP₂
Akt directly phosphorylates Ras-GEF to amplify the parallel Ras/MAPK signal
questions.explain: Akt does not phosphorylate Ras-GEF. The PI3K/Akt and Ras/MAPK pathways are parallel branches activated by different phosphotyrosine docking sites on the RTK; Akt promotes cell survival and growth by phosphorylating its own distinct set of targets, not by feeding into the Ras pathway.

questions.q: EGFR is activated by EGF binding. Which two major downstream branches are simultaneously activated by the single activated EGFR dimer?
options: The adenylyl cyclase/cAMP/PKA branch and the PLC/IP₃/Ca²⁺ branch
The Ras/MAPK branch and the PI3K/Akt branch
The guanylyl cyclase/cGMP branch and the serine/threonine kinase branch
The Wnt/β-catenin branch and the Notch/NICD branch
The JAK/STAT branch and the Smad/TGF-β branch
questions.explain: EGFR simultaneously activates both the Ras/MAPK pathway (via Grb2–SOS docking on one phosphotyrosine) and the PI3K/Akt pathway (via PI3K docking on a different phosphotyrosine). This multiplexing from a single receptor is a hallmark of RTK signaling, not a feature of GPCR signaling.

questions.q: A patient's tumor cells express EGFRvIII, a mutant EGFR lacking the extracellular domain. Which statement about EGFRvIII is correct?
options: EGFRvIII requires higher-than-normal EGF concentrations to be activated
EGFRvIII cannot dimerize and therefore has reduced signaling activity
EGFRvIII is constitutively active because it lacks the extracellular ligand-binding domain
EGFRvIII activates only the PI3K/Akt pathway because the Ras-docking site is deleted
EGFRvIII is sensitive to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies that target the extracellular domain
questions.explain: EGFRvIII is a deletion mutant that lacks the extracellular ligand-binding domain. Without this domain, the receptor can no longer be regulated by ligand, and it signals constitutively — driving uncontrolled proliferation. Because EGFRvIII lacks an extracellular domain, anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (which target the extracellular domain) would not bind or inhibit it.

questions.q: How do EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) mechanistically differ from anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies?
options: TKIs block the extracellular ligand-binding domain; mAbs block the intracellular kinase domain
TKIs block the intracellular ATP-binding site of the kinase domain; mAbs block the extracellular domain
TKIs degrade EGFR mRNA; mAbs bind EGFR protein and trigger phagocytosis
TKIs activate PTEN to dephosphorylate PIP₃; mAbs block Grb2 from docking on phosphotyrosines
TKIs and mAbs both block the extracellular domain but through different binding epitopes
questions.explain: TKIs are small molecules that compete with ATP for the intracellular kinase domain, preventing autophosphorylation. Anti-EGFR mAbs are large proteins that bind the extracellular domain, blocking EGF binding and/or receptor dimerization. They target different sites on the same receptor molecule.

questions.q: Which acquired resistance mutation arises in EGFR after treatment with first- or second-generation TKIs, and which generation of TKIs was designed to overcome it?
options: C797S mutation arises; 2nd generation TKIs (Afatinib) overcome it
T790M mutation arises; 3rd generation TKIs (e.g., Osimertinib/AZD9291) overcome it
T790M mutation arises; 4th generation TKIs (EAI045) overcome it
EGFRvIII deletion arises; 3rd generation TKIs overcome it
C797S mutation arises; 1st generation TKIs overcome it after dose escalation
questions.explain: First- and second-generation EGFR TKIs (e.g., Gefitinib, Erlotinib, Afatinib) are effective until tumors acquire the T790M resistance mutation. Third-generation TKIs such as Osimertinib (AZD9291) were specifically designed to target T790M-mutant EGFR. Resistance to 3rd-generation TKIs then emerges via the C797S mutation, which is the target of 4th-generation TKIs like EAI045.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the G1/S checkpoint in the cell cycle?
options: To ensure DNA replication begins at the correct time
To verify the cell is ready for DNA replication and has undamaged DNA
To check that mitosis is complete
To allow the cell to exit the cell cycle
To initiate cytokinesis
questions.explain: The G1/S checkpoint assesses whether conditions are favorable for DNA replication and whether DNA damage is present. It's a critical gate preventing damaged DNA from being replicated.

questions.q: Which cyclin-CDK complex drives the G1/S transition?
options: Cyclin D-CDK4/6
Cyclin E-CDK2
Cyclin A-CDK2
Cyclin B-CDK1
Cyclin-dependent kinase 7
questions.explain: Cyclin E-CDK2 is responsible for pushing the cell from G1 into S phase and initiating DNA replication. Cyclin D-CDK4/6 acts earlier in G1.

questions.q: What triggers sister chromatids to separate during anaphase?
options: Degradation of the nuclear envelope
Activation of the spindle checkpoint
Destruction of cohesins by the APC
Activation of condensins
Reformation of the nuclear envelope
questions.explain: Sister chromatids are held together by cohesin proteins. The APC ubiquitin ligase targets cohesins for degradation, allowing sister chromatids to separate and be pulled to opposite poles.

questions.q: How much DNA must be compacted during prophase in human cells?
options: 2 millimeters
2 centimeters
2 meters
2 micrometers
2 nanometers
questions.explain: Human DNA totals approximately 2 meters in length but must fit into a nucleus with a radius of only ~10 µm. Condensins facilitate this extreme compaction.

questions.q: What is the result of homologous recombination during meiosis I?
options: Production of four identical haploid cells
Exchange of genetic material between maternal and paternal chromosomes
Separation of sister chromatids
Doubling of the chromosome number
Prevention of genetic variation
questions.explain: Crossing-over between homologous chromosomes during meiosis I allows maternal and paternal DNA to recombine, creating new allele combinations and increasing genetic diversity.

questions.q: How many meiotic divisions are required to produce four haploid cells?
options: One division
Two divisions
Three divisions
Four divisions
No divisions—meiosis is asexual
questions.explain: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (producing two diploid cells), and meiosis II separates sister chromatids (producing four haploid cells). Two divisions total.

questions.q: What is the primary evolutionary advantage of sexual reproduction?
options: It is faster than asexual reproduction
It produces identical offspring
It generates genetic diversity providing a competitive advantage in changing environments
It requires less energy
It eliminates the need for mitosis
questions.explain: Sexual reproduction mixes parental chromosomes and introduces recombination, creating genetic variation. This diversity helps populations adapt to environmental changes.

questions.q: Which statement about Mendel's law of segregation is correct?
options: Alleles remain together throughout an organism's life
Alleles separate during meiosis and reunite randomly at fertilization
Alleles can never separate from each other
All offspring receive identical alleles from both parents
Segregation occurs during mitosis
questions.explain: The law of segregation states that the two alleles for each trait separate during meiosis, with each gamete receiving one allele. They reunite randomly during fertilization.

questions.q: What is a consequence of nondisjunction during meiosis?
options: Production of four haploid cells with one extra chromosome each
Production of gametes with missing or extra chromosomes
Immediate death of the organism
Perfect chromosome segregation
Increased genetic recombination
questions.explain: Nondisjunction is the failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate. This produces some gametes with extra chromosomes and others with missing chromosomes. Fertilization with such gametes causes aneuploidy.

questions.q: Down syndrome results from which chromosomal abnormality?
options: Monosomy 21
Trisomy 21
Tetrasomy 21
Deletion of chromosome 21
Inversion of chromosome 21
questions.explain: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) occurs when an individual inherits three copies of chromosome 21 instead of the normal two, usually due to nondisjunction during meiosis.

questions.q: What is the function of kinetochores during mitosis?
options: To synthesize new DNA
To guide chromosome condensation
To attach chromosomes to spindle microtubules
To disassemble the nuclear envelope
To produce spindle poles
questions.explain: Kinetochores are protein structures at centromeres that attach chromosomes to spindle microtubules, allowing the spindle to pull sister chromatids apart during anaphase.

questions.q: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes first become visible under a light microscope?
options: Interphase
Prophase
Metaphase
Anaphase
Telophase
questions.explain: Chromosomes condense during prophase, making them visible as distinct structures for the first time. Prior to prophase, DNA is loosely packaged as chromatin.

questions.q: What is the consequence of a G2/M checkpoint failure?
options: Damaged DNA is replicated, spreading mutations
Cell cycle arrest and apoptosis
Immediate production of two daughter cells
Formation of extra centrosomes
Prevention of cytokinesis
questions.explain: The G2/M checkpoint prevents entry into mitosis if DNA replication is incomplete or damage is detected. Failure of this checkpoint allows damaged DNA to be segregated into daughter cells.

questions.q: Which protein complex triggers cyclin degradation?
options: Topoisomerase
Helicase
The anaphase-promoting complex (APC)
DNA polymerase
Primase
questions.explain: The APC is an ubiquitin ligase that polyubiquitinates cyclins, marking them for proteasomal degradation. This removes cyclins and inactivates CDKs, allowing cell cycle progression.

questions.q: What is the relationship between cyclins and CDKs?
options: CDKs inhibit cyclins
Cyclins activate CDKs by binding to them
CDKs activate cyclins
Cyclins and CDKs are the same protein
They have no functional relationship
questions.explain: CDKs are catalytically inactive without cyclin binding partners. When cyclins accumulate and bind, they activate CDKs, allowing them to phosphorylate target proteins and drive cell cycle events.

questions.q: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis I?
options: Mitosis produces haploid cells; meiosis I produces diploid cells
Mitosis separates sister chromatids; meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes
Mitosis involves genetic recombination; meiosis I does not
Mitosis occurs only in germ cells; meiosis I occurs in somatic cells
There is no difference—they are identical processes
questions.explain: In mitosis, sister chromatids separate, maintaining ploidy. In meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate, reducing ploidy from diploid to haploid.

questions.q: How many unique gametes can a human produce theoretically?
options: 2^23 (approximately 8.4 million)
2^46 (approximately 70 trillion)
Infinite without limit
questions.explain: With 23 chromosome pairs, each of which can assort independently into gametes, humans can produce 2^23 ≈ 8.4 million genetically unique gametes.

questions.q: What is the purpose of cohesins during meiosis I?
options: To separate homologous chromosomes
To initiate crossing-over
To glue sister chromatids together and ensure proper bivalent segregation
To degrade at the metaphase/anaphase checkpoint
To form the spindle poles
questions.explain: Cohesins hold sister chromatids and homologous chromosomes together. Chiasmata (the sites of crossing-over) are maintained by cohesins, ensuring homologs don't separate prematurely.

questions.q: What is a bivalent in meiosis?
options: Two sister chromatids
Two homologous chromosomes that have paired up
Four haploid cells
The spindle structure
A pair of centrosomes
questions.explain: During meiosis I, homologous chromosomes synapse (pair up) to form a bivalent. Each bivalent consists of four chromatids (two sister chromatids from each homolog).

questions.q: During which stage of meiosis do sister chromatids separate?
options: Meiosis I prophase
Meiosis I anaphase
Meiosis II prophase
Meiosis II anaphase
During cytokinesis
questions.explain: Sister chromatids remain together through meiosis I. They separate during anaphase II, similar to mitotic anaphase.

questions.q: What does the law of independent assortment describe?
options: Segregation of alleles for a single gene
Random segregation of genes on different chromosomes
Bonding between sister chromatids
Recombination within a chromosome
Always producing offspring with parental phenotypes
questions.explain: Independent assortment refers to the random, independent segregation of different genes (especially those on different chromosomes) during gamete formation.

questions.q: What is the significance of chiasmata in meiosis?
options: They are failure points where crossing-over never occurs
They are the sites of crossing-over where genetic recombination occurs
They are spindle pole structures
They prevent homologous recombination
They separate sister chromatids
questions.explain: Chiasmata are the visible points where crossing-over has occurred between homologous chromosomes. They represent sites of genetic recombination and are held together by cohesins.

questions.q: A monohybrid cross between two heterozygotes (Aa × Aa) produces which ratio in offspring?
options: All homozygous
questions.explain: In an Aa × Aa cross, the F2 generation shows a 3:1 phenotypic ratio (three dominant to one recessive), reflecting the segregation of alleles according to Mendel's first law.

questions.q: What is the function of condensins during mitosis?
options: To replicate DNA
To facilitate chromosome condensation from chromatin
To attach spindle microtubules to chromosomes
To trigger cyclin degradation
To form the contractile ring
questions.explain: Condensins are protein complexes that compact chromatin into visible condensed chromosomes during prophase, allowing the ~2 meters of human DNA to fit within a ~10 µm nucleus.

questions.q: What event marks the start of cytokinesis in animal cells?
options: Separation of sister chromatids
Dissolution of the nuclear envelope
Formation of a contractile ring at the cell equator
Degradation of cyclin B
Activation of the spindle checkpoint
questions.explain: Cytokinesis begins when a contractile ring of actin and myosin forms at the cell's equator. Tightening of this ring divides the cytoplasm, producing two daughter cells.

questions.q: Which checkpoint ensures that all chromosomes are properly attached to spindle microtubules?
options: G1/S checkpoint
G2/M checkpoint
Metaphase/anaphase transition checkpoint
S phase checkpoint
Telophase checkpoint
questions.explain: The metaphase/anaphase checkpoint (also called the spindle checkpoint) verifies that all kinetochores are attached to spindle microtubules before allowing anaphase to proceed.

questions.q: What is aneuploidy?
options: Normal chromosome number
Abnormal number of chromosomes (not the normal diploid or haploid amount)
Loss of all chromosomes
Duplication of a single gene
Separation of sister chromatids
questions.explain: Aneuploidy is the condition of having an abnormal number of chromosomes, usually resulting from nondisjunction. Examples include trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) and monosomy X (Turner syndrome).

questions.q: In the dihybrid cross YyRr × YyRr, what phenotypic ratio is predicted?
questions.explain: In a dihybrid cross between two heterozygotes for two independently assorting genes, the predicted F2 phenotypic ratio is 9:3:3:1 (nine dominant for both traits, three dominant for each single trait, one double recessive).

questions.q: What is sickle-cell anemia caused by?
options: A chromosomal deletion
A single nucleotide mutation in the β-globin gene causing abnormal hemoglobin
Nondisjunction during meiosis
Loss of an entire chromosome
Failure of DNA replication
questions.explain: Sickle-cell anemia results from a point mutation that changes one amino acid in β-globin, causing hemoglobin polymerization and red blood cell sickling under low oxygen conditions.

questions.q: What occurs during the S phase of the cell cycle?
options: Cell division
DNA replication
Protein degradation
Chromosome condensation
Spindle assembly
questions.explain: S phase (synthesis phase) is when DNA replication occurs, doubling the genetic material in preparation for cell division.

lectures.title: Lecture 20: Sexual Reproduction & The Power of Genetics

questions.q: What is the fundamental difference between asexual and sexual reproduction?
options: Asexual uses meiosis; sexual uses mitosis
Asexual produces genetically identical offspring from one parent; sexual combines genetic material from two parents
Sexual reproduction is faster than asexual
Asexual organisms are haploid; sexual organisms are diploid
They are identical processes
questions.explain: Asexual reproduction (like bacterial cell division) produces genetically identical clones. Sexual reproduction involves meiosis and fertilization, combining genetic material from two parents and creating genetic diversity.

questions.q: How many cell divisions occur during meiosis, and what is the ploidy of the resulting cells?
options: One division producing diploid cells
Two divisions producing diploid cells
Two divisions producing haploid cells
Three divisions producing haploid cells
One division producing haploid cells
questions.explain: Meiosis involves two sequential divisions. Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes; meiosis II separates sister chromatids. The result is four haploid cells.

questions.q: What is homologous recombination, and when does it occur?
options: Separation of sister chromatids during meiosis II
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during meiosis I
Replication of DNA during S phase
Assembly of the spindle during prophase
Degradation of cyclins during mitosis
questions.explain: Homologous recombination (crossing-over) occurs during meiosis I when paired homologous chromosomes exchange segments of DNA, creating new combinations of alleles and increasing genetic diversity.

questions.q: What is a major source of genetic variation during sexual reproduction?
options: DNA replication errors
Mitosis in somatic cells
Independent assortment and crossing-over
Cytokinesis failures
Apoptosis of cells
questions.explain: Independent assortment randomly distributes maternal and paternal chromosomes into gametes. Crossing-over creates new allele combinations within individual chromosomes. Together, they generate enormous genetic variation.

questions.q: What is the significance of independent assortment in a dihybrid cross?
options: Both traits are inherited from the same parent
Genes for different traits segregate independently
Prevents genetic diversity
Occurs only in prokaryotes
Allows sister chromatids to separate
questions.explain: Independent assortment states that genes on different chromosomes segregate independently during meiosis, producing a 9:3:3:1 ratio in the F2 generation of a dihybrid cross.

questions.q: What is Mendel's law of segregation?
options: Genes are always linked on the same chromosome
Alleles separate during meiosis and reunite randomly at fertilization
All offspring are heterozygous
Mutations occur during meiosis
Chromosomes never separate
questions.explain: The law of segregation describes how the two alleles for each trait separate during meiosis, with each gamete receiving one allele. At fertilization, alleles reunite randomly, producing various genotypes.

questions.q: In the F2 generation of a monohybrid Aa × Aa cross, what is the phenotypic ratio?
options: All dominant
questions.explain: The monohybrid cross Aa × Aa produces offspring in a 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa genotypic ratio, resulting in a 3:1 phenotypic ratio (three dominant to one recessive).

questions.q: What is a benefit of sexual reproduction in an unpredictable environment?
options: Faster population growth
Identical offspring for consistency
Genetic diversity provides a competitive advantage for survival
Reduced energy expenditure
Simplified inheritance patterns
questions.explain: Sexual reproduction generates genetic diversity through recombination and independent assortment. This variation allows populations to adapt to environmental changes and helps eliminate deleterious genes.

questions.q: What is a consequence of a nondisjunction event during meiosis?
options: Production of four normal haploid cells
Production of some gametes with extra or missing chromosomes
Increased crossing-over between chromosomes
Failure of centrosome duplication
Activation of the G1/S checkpoint
questions.explain: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis. This produces some gametes with extra chromosomes and others missing chromosomes.

questions.q: Down syndrome results from trisomy 21. What is the consequence for the affected individual?
options: Increased fertility
Intellectual disability and specific physical characteristics
Lethality in utero
Increased resistance to disease
No observable effects
questions.explain: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is caused by three copies of chromosome 21, usually from nondisjunction during meiosis. It results in intellectual disability, short stature, and distinctive facial features.

questions.q: What is the relationship between meiosis and the production of gametes?
options: Meiosis produces diploid somatic cells
Meiosis produces haploid gametes (sperm and eggs)
Meiosis is another name for mitosis
Gametes are produced by mitosis
Meiosis has no role in reproduction
questions.explain: Meiosis is the specialized cell division that produces haploid gametes from diploid germ-line cells, enabling sexual reproduction.

questions.q: What role do chiasmata play in meiosis I?
options: They prevent genetic recombination
They are sites where crossing-over has occurred, held together by cohesins to ensure proper chromosome segregation
They initiate DNA replication
They separate sister chromatids
They form the mitotic spindle
questions.explain: Chiasmata are the visible structures at sites where crossing-over (homologous recombination) has occurred. They are held together by cohesins and help ensure proper segregation of homologous chromosomes.

questions.q: How many genetically distinct gametes can a human with 23 chromosomes theoretically produce?
options: 2^23 (approximately 8.4 million)
Infinite
questions.explain: With 23 chromosome pairs, each assorted independently, humans can produce 2^23 ≈ 8.4 million unique gametes, giving enormous potential for genetic diversity.

questions.q: What is the consequence of fertilization combining two genetically unique gametes?
options: All offspring are identical clones
Genetic diversity in the offspring population
Sterility of the offspring
Immediate mutation of the zygote
No genetic change compared to parental types
questions.explain: Fertilization randomly combines two unique gametes, further increasing genetic variation. Even with the same two parents, offspring are genetically distinct.

questions.q: What distinguishes mitosis from meiosis I in terms of chromosome behavior?
options: Both separate sister chromatids
Mitosis separates sister chromatids; meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes
Meiosis I separates sister chromatids; mitosis separates homologous chromosomes
They are identical processes
Neither separates any chromosomes
questions.explain: Mitosis separates sister chromatids, maintaining chromosome number. Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, reducing the number by half.

questions.q: What is the function of crossing-over during meiosis I?
options: To increase chromosome number
To prevent genetic variation
To exchange genetic material between homologous chromosomes, creating new allele combinations
To trigger DNA replication
To separate sister chromatids
questions.explain: Crossing-over exchanges segments of DNA between homologous chromosomes, creating recombinant chromosomes with new combinations of alleles and increasing genetic diversity.

questions.q: What is a temperature-sensitive mutant used for in genetic research?
options: To increase mutation rates
To identify genes controlling essential cellular processes
To produce heat-resistant organisms
To prevent crossing-over
To accelerate meiosis
questions.explain: Temperature-sensitive mutants function normally at permissive temperatures but are defective at restrictive temperatures, making them valuable for identifying genes required for specific essential processes.

questions.q: What is Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) primarily used for?
options: Breeding organisms for desired traits
Replicating DNA quickly
Rapidly sequencing and analyzing genomes to identify mutations and genetic abnormalities
Performing meiosis
Degrading chromosomes
questions.explain: NGS enables rapid, cost-effective sequencing of large DNA samples, allowing researchers to identify disease-associated mutations, detect genetic abnormalities, and track pathogen outbreaks.

questions.q: What genetic change causes sickle-cell anemia?
options: Loss of an entire chromosome
A single nucleotide mutation in the β-globin gene
Nondisjunction during meiosis
Deletion of the hemoglobin gene
Duplication of chromosome 21
questions.explain: Sickle-cell anemia results from a point mutation in the β-globin gene that changes the amino acid glutamic acid to valine, causing abnormal hemoglobin polymerization and red blood cell sickling.

questions.q: How does a bivalent form during meiosis I?
options: Two sister chromatids remain attached
Homologous chromosomes synapse (pair up) to form a structure with four chromatids
Four centrosomes align at the equator
The spindle separates DNA strands
Sister chromatids exchange genetic material
questions.explain: During meiosis I prophase, homologous chromosomes synapse to form a bivalent, a structure consisting of four chromatids (two sister chromatids from each homolog). Crossing-over occurs between homologs within the bivalent.

questions.q: What is the key difference in how independent assortment and segregation operate?
options: Segregation applies to all genes; independent assortment only to linked genes
Segregation separates alleles for one gene; independent assortment involves different genes segregating independently
They are identical processes
Segregation occurs during mitosis; independent assortment during meiosis only
Independent assortment prevents genetic diversity
questions.explain: Segregation refers to the separation of alleles for a single gene. Independent assortment refers to the independent segregation of different genes, especially those on different chromosomes.

questions.q: What is the relationship between nondisjunction and aneuploidy?
options: They are unrelated
Nondisjunction (failure of chromosome separation) causes aneuploidy (abnormal chromosome number)
Aneuploidy causes nondisjunction
Both only occur in prokaryotes
Neither affects gametes
questions.explain: Nondisjunction is the failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate during meiosis. This directly causes aneuploidy, producing gametes with extra or missing chromosomes.

questions.q: What is the significance of genetic variation in a population facing environmental stress?
options: Genetic variation has no impact
Populations with high genetic diversity are more likely to include individuals with traits beneficial for survival
All individuals are equally likely to survive
Genetic variation increases mutation rates
Environmental stress reduces genetic diversity
questions.explain: Genetic diversity increases the probability that a population includes individuals with traits suited to new environmental conditions, improving survival and adaptation.

questions.q: In a cross between a homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive individual (AA × aa), what is the genotype of all F1 offspring?
options: All AA
All aa
All Aa
1/2 AA and 1/2 aa
Mixed genotypes
questions.explain: In a cross between homozygous dominant (AA) and homozygous recessive (aa), all F1 offspring are heterozygous (Aa) and express the dominant phenotype.

questions.q: What happens during metaphase II of meiosis?
options: Homologous chromosomes align at the equator
Sister chromatids align at the equator
The nuclear envelope breaks down and sister chromatids separate
Cyclins are degraded
Centrosomes duplicate
questions.explain: During metaphase II, the cell is haploid (half the chromosome number of the original diploid cell). Individual chromosomes (each consisting of two sister chromatids) align at the equator, similar to metaphase of mitosis.

questions.q: What is the role of germ-line cells in sexual reproduction?
options: To produce somatic cells
To undergo meiosis and produce gametes (sperm and eggs)
To produce mitochondria
To prevent genetic variation
To synthesize proteins
questions.explain: Germ-line cells are specialized cells (diploid) that undergo meiosis to produce haploid gametes. Somatic cells, by contrast, undergo mitosis for growth and repair.

questions.q: What is the primary outcome of sexual reproduction compared to asexual reproduction?
options: Faster growth
Reduced offspring number
Increased genetic diversity
Simpler inheritance
Lower mutation rates
questions.explain: Sexual reproduction combines genetic material from two parents and involves recombination, creating offspring with novel genetic combinations unavailable in asexual reproduction.

questions.q: During meiosis II, which cellular structures separate?
options: Homologous chromosomes
Sister chromatids
Centrosomes
Spindle poles
Centrioles
questions.explain: Meiosis II is similar to mitosis. Sister chromatids, which were held together by cohesins, separate and move to opposite poles.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the APC during mitosis and meiosis?
options: To synthesize cyclin proteins
To attach chromosomes to spindle

questions.explain: The anaphase-promoting complex (APC) is an ubiquitin ligase that marks cohesins and cyclins for degradation, triggering anaphase and regulating cell cycle progression.

questions.q: What evolutionary advantage does meiosis provide over asexual reproduction?
options: Faster cell division
More offspring per generation
Genetic diversity enabling population adaptation to change
Simpler genetic inheritance
Reduced metabolic cost
questions.explain: Meiosis creates genetic diversity through recombination and independent assortment. This variation allows populations to adapt to environmental changes and increases evolutionary fitness.
